nanstr=None,\\n116:                      infstr=None, formatter=None, sign=None, floatmode=None,\\n117:                      *, legacy=None, override_repr=None):\\n118:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n119:     Set printing options.\\n120: \\n121:     These options determine the way floating point numbers, arrays and\\n122:     other NumPy objects are displayed.\\n123: \\n124:     Parameters\\n125:     ----------\\n126:     precision : int or None, optional\\n127:         Number of digits of precision for floating point output (default 8).\\n128:         May be None if `floatmode` is not `fixed`, to print as many digits as\\n129:         necessary to uniquely specify the value.\\n130:     threshold : int, optional\\n131:         Total number of array elements which trigger summarization\\n132:         rather than full repr (default 1000).\\n133:         To always use the full repr without summarization, pass `sys.maxsize`.\\n134:     edgeitems : int, optional\\n135:         Number of array items in summary at beginning and end of\\n136:         each dimension (default 3).\\n137:     linewidth : int, optional\\n138:         The number of characters per line for the purpose of inserting\\n139:         line breaks (default 75).\\n140:     suppress : bool, optional\\n141:         If True, always print floating point numbers using fixed point\\n142:         notation, in which case numbers equal to zero in the current precision\\n143:         will print as zero.  If False, then scientific notation is used when\\n144:         absolute value of the smallest number is < 1e-4 or the ratio of the\\n145:         maximum absolute value to the minimum is > 1e3. The default is False.\\n146:     nanstr : str, optional\\n147:         String representation of floating point not-a-number (default nan).\\n148:     infstr : str, optional\\n149:         String representation of floating point infinity (default inf).\\n150:     sign : string, either '-', '+', or ' ', optional\\n151:         Controls printing of the sign of floating-point types. If '+', always\\n152:         print the sign of positive values. If ' ', always prints a space\\n153:         (whitespace character) in the sign position of positive values.  If\\n154:         '-', omit the sign character of positive values. (default '-')\\n155: \\n156:         .. versionchanged:: 2.0\\n157:              The sign parameter can now be an integer type, previously\\n158:              types were floating-point types.\\n159: \\n160:     formatter : dict of callables, optional\\n161:         If not None, the keys should indicate the type(s) that the respective\\n162:         formatting function applies to.  Callables should return a string.\\n163:         Types that are not specified (by their corresponding keys) are handled\\n164:         by the default formatters.  Individual types for which a formatter\\n165:         can be set are:\\n166: \\n167:         - 'bool'\\n168:         - 'int'\\n169:         - 'timedelta' : a `numpy.timedelta64`\\n170:         - 'datetime' : a `numpy.datetime64`\\n171:         - 'float'\\n172:         - 'longfloat' : 128-bit floats\\n173:         - 'complexfloat'\\n174:         - 'longcomplexfloat' : composed of two 128-bit floats\\n175:         - 'numpystr' : types `numpy.bytes_` and `numpy.str_`\\n176:         - 'object' : `np.object_` arrays\\n177: \\n178:         Other keys that can be used to set a group of types at once are:\\n179: \\n180:         - 'all' : sets all types\\n181:         - 'int_kind' : sets 'int'\\n182:         - 'float_kind' : sets 'float' and 'longfloat'\\n183:         - 'complex_kind' : sets 'complexfloat' and 'longcomplexfloat'\\n184:         - 'str_kind' : sets 'numpystr'\\n185:     floatmode : str, optional\\n186:         Controls the interpretation of the `precision` option for\\n187:         floating-point types. Can take the following values\\n188:         (default maxprec_equal):\\n189: \\n190:         * 'fixed': Always print exactly `precision` fractional digits,\\n191:                 even if this would print more or fewer digits than\\n192:                 necessary to specify the value uniquely.\\n193:         * 'unique': Print the minimum number of fractional digits necessary\\n194:                 to represent each value uniquely. Different elements may\\n195:                 have a different number of digits. The value of the\\n196:                 `precision` option is ignored.\\n197:         * 'maxprec': Print at most `precision` fractional digits, but if\\n198:                 an element can be uniquely represented with fewer digits\\n199:                 only print it with that many.\\n200:         * 'maxprec_equal': Print at most `precision` fractional digits,\\n201:                 but if every element in the array can be uniquely\\n202:                 represented with an equal number of fewer digits, use that\\n203:                 many digits for all elements.\\n204:     legacy : string or `False`, optional\\n205:         If set to the string ``'1.13'`` enables 1.13 legacy printing mode. This\\n206:         approximates numpy 1.13 print output by including a space in the sign\\n207:         position of floats and different behavior for 0d arrays. This also\\n208:         enables 1.21 legacy printing mode (described below).\\n209: \\n210:         If set to the string ``'1.21'`` enables 1.21 legacy printing mode. This\\n211:         approximates numpy 1.21 print output of complex structured dtypes\\n212:         by not inserting spaces after commas that separate fields and after\\n213:         colons.\\n214: \\n215:         If set to ``'1.25'`` approximates printing of 1.25 which mainly means\\n216:         that numeric scalars are printed without their type information, e.g.\\n217:         as ``3.0`` rather than ``np.float64(3.0)``.\\n218: \\n219:         If set to ``'2.1'``, shape information is not given when arrays are\\n220:         summarized (i.e., multiple elements replaced with ``...``).\\n221: \\n222:         If set to `False`, disables legacy mode.\\n223: \\n224:         Unrecognized strings will be ignored with a warning for forward\\n225:         compatibility.\\n226: \\n227:         .. versionchanged:: 1.22.0\\n228:         .. versionchanged:: 2.2\\n229: \\n230:     override_repr: callable, optional\\n231:         If set a passed function will be used for generating arrays' repr.\\n232:         Other options will be ignored.\\n233: \\n234:     See Also\\n235:     --------\\n236:     get_printoptions, printoptions, array2string\\n237: \\n238:     Notes\\n239:     -----\\n240:     `formatter` is always reset with a call to `set_printoptions`.\\n241: \\n242:     Use `printoptions` as a context manager to set the values temporarily.\\n243: \\n244:     Examples\\n245:     --------\\n246:     Floating point precision can be set:\\n247: \\n248:     >>> import numpy as np\\n249:     >>> np.set_printoptions(precision=4)\\n250:     >>> np.array([1.123456789])\\n251:     [1.1235]\\n252: \\n253:     Long arrays can be summarised:\\n254: \\n255:     >>> np.set_printoptions(threshold=5)\\n256:     >>> np.arange(10)\\n257:     array([0, 1, 2, ..., 7, 8, 9], shape=(10,))\\n258: \\n259:     Small results can be suppressed:\\n260: \\n261:     >>> eps = np.finfo(float).eps\\n262:     >>> x = np.arange(4.)\\n263:     >>> x**2 - (x + eps)**2\\n264:     array([-4.9304e-32, -4.4409e-16,  0.0000e+00,  0.0000e+00])\\n265:     >>> np.set_printoptions(suppress=True)\\n266:     >>> x**2 - (x + eps)**2\\n267:     array([-0., -0.,  0.,  0.])\\n268: \\n269:     A custom formatter can be used to display array elements as desired:\\n270: \\n271:     >>> np.set_printoptions(formatter={'all':lambda x: 'int: '+str(-x)})\\n272:     >>> x = np.arange(3)\\n273:     >>> x\\n274:     array([int: 0, int: -1, int: -2])\\n275:     >>> np.set_printoptions()  # formatter gets reset\\n276:     >>> x\\n277:     array([0, 1, 2])\\n278: \\n279:     To put back the default options, you can use:\\n280: \\n281:     >>> np.set_printoptions(edgeitems=3, infstr='inf',\\n282:     ... linewidth=75, nanstr='nan', precision=8,\\n283:     ... suppress=False, threshold=1000, formatter=None)\\n284: \\n285:     Also to temporarily override options, use `printoptions`\\n286:     as a context manager:\\n287: \\n288:     >>> with np.printoptions(precision=2, suppress=True, threshold=5):\\n289:     ...     np.linspace(0, 10, 10)\\n290:     array([ 0.  ,  1.11,  2.22, ...,  7.78,  8.89, 10.  ], shape=(10,))\\n291: \\n292:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n293:     _set_printoptions(precision, threshold, edgeitems, linewidth, suppress,\\n294:                       nanstr, infstr, formatter, sign, floatmode,\\n295:                       legacy=legacy, override_repr=override_repr)\\n296: \\n297: \\n298: def _set_printoptions(precision=None, threshold=None, edgeitems=None,\\n299:                       linewidth=None, suppress=None, nanstr=None,\\n300:                       infstr=None, formatter=None, sign=None, floatmode=None,\\n301:                       *, legacy=None, override_repr=None):\\n302:     new_opt = _make_options_dict(precision, threshold, edgeitems, linewidth,\\n303:                                  suppress, nanstr, infstr, sign, formatter,\\n304:                                  floatmode, legacy)\\n305:     # formatter and override_repr are always reset\\n306:     new_opt['formatter'] = formatter\\n307:     new_opt['override_repr'] = override_repr\\n308: \\n309:     updated_opt = format_options.get() | new_opt\\n310:     updated_opt.update(new_opt)\\n311: \\n312:     if updated_opt['legacy'] == 113:\\n313:         updated_opt['sign'] = '-'\\n314: \\n315:     return format_options.set(updated_opt)\\n316: \\n317: \\n318: @set_module('numpy')\\n319: def get_printoptions():\\n320:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n321:     Return the current print options.\\n322: \\n323:     Returns\\n324:     -------\\n325:     print_opts : dict\\n326:         Dictionary of current print options with keys\\n327: \\n328:         - precision : int\\n329:         - threshold : int\\n330:         - edgeitems : int\\n331:         - linewidth : int\\n332:         - suppress : bool\\n333:         - nanstr : str\\n334:         - infstr : str\\n335:         - sign : str\\n336:         - formatter : dict of callables\\n337:         - floatmode : str\\n338:         - legacy : str or False\\n339: \\n340:         For a full description of these options, see `set_printoptions`.\\n341: \\n342:     See Also\\n343:     --------\\n344:     set_printoptions, printoptions\\n345: \\n346:     Examples\\n347:     --------\\n348:     >>> import numpy as np\\n349: \\n350:     >>> np.get_printoptions()\\n351:     {'edgeitems': 3, 'threshold': 1000, ..., 'override_repr': None}\\n352: \\n353:     >>> np.get_printoptions()['linewidth']\\n354:     75\\n355:     >>> np.set_printoptions(linewidth=100)\\n356:     >>> np.get_printoptions()['linewidth']\\n357:     100\\n358: \\n359:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n360:     opts = format_options.get().copy()\\n361:     opts['legacy'] = {\\n362:         113: '1.13', 121: '1.21', 125: '1.25', sys.maxsize: False,\\n363:     }[opts['legacy']]\\n364:     return opts\\n365: \\n366: \\n367: def _get_legacy_print_mode():\\n368:     \\\"\\\"\\\"Return the legacy print mode as an int.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n369:     return format_options.get()['legacy']\\n370: \\n371: \\n372: @set_module('numpy')\\n373: @contextlib.contextmanager\\n374: def printoptions(*args, **kwargs):\\n375:     \\\"\\\"\\\"Context manager for setting print options.\\n376: \\n377:     Set print options for the scope of the `with` block, and restore the old\\n378:     options at the end. See `set_printoptions` for the full description of\\n379:     available options.\\n380: \\n381:     Examples\\n382:     --------\\n383:     >>> import numpy as np\\n384: \\n385:     >>> from numpy.testing import assert_equal\\n386:     >>> with np.printoptions(precision=2):\\n387:     ...     np.array([2.0]) / 3\\n388:     array([0.67])\\n389: \\n390:     The `as`-clause of the `with`-statement gives the current print options:\\n391: \\n392:     >>> with np.printoptions(precision=2) as opts:\\n393:     ...      assert_equal(opts, np.get_printoptions())\\n394: \\n395:     See Also\\n396:     --------\\n397:     set_printoptions, get_printoptions\\n398: \\n399:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n400:     token = _set_printoptions(*args, **kwargs)\\n401: \\n402:     try:\\n403:         yield get_printoptions()\\n404:     finally:\\n405:         format_options.reset(token)\\n406: \\n407: \\n408: def _leading_trailing(a, edgeitems, index=()):\\n409:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n410:     Keep only the N-D corners (leading and trailing edges) of an array.\\n411: \\n412:     Should be passed a base-class ndarray, since it makes no guarantees about\\n413:     preserving subclasses.\\n414:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n415:     axis = len(index)\\n416:     if axis == a.ndim:\\n417:         return a[index]\\n418: \\n419:     if a.shape[axis] > 2*edgeitems:\\n420:         return concatenate((\\n421:             _leading_trailing(a, edgeitems, index + np.index_exp[:edgeitems]),\\n422:             _leading_trailing(a, edgeitems, index + np.index_exp[-edgeitems:])\\n423:         ), axis=axis)\\n424:     else:\\n425:         return _leading_trailing(a, edgeitems, index + np.index_exp[:])\\n426: \\n427: \\n428: def _object_format(o):\\n429:     \\\"\\\"\\\" Object arrays containing lists should be printed unambiguously \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n430:     if type(o) is list:\\n431:         fmt = 'list({!r})'\\n432:     else:\\n433:         fmt = '{!r}'\\n434:     return fmt.format(o)\\n435: \\n436: def repr_format(x):\\n437:     if isinstance(x, (np.str_, np.bytes_)):\\n438:         return repr(x.item())\\n439:     return repr(x)\\n440: \\n441: def str_format(x):\\n442:     if isinstance(x, (np.str_, np.bytes_)):\\n443:         return str(x.item())\\n444:     return str(x)\\n445: \\n446: def _get_formatdict(data, *, precision, floatmode, suppress, sign, legacy,\\n447:                     formatter, **kwargs):\\n448:     # note: extra arguments in kwargs are ignored\\n449: \\n450:     # wrapped in lambdas to avoid taking a code path\\n451:     # with the wrong type of data\\n452:     formatdict = {\\n453:         'bool': lambda: BoolFormat(data),\\n454:         'int': lambda: IntegerFormat(data, sign),\\n455:         'float': lambda: FloatingFormat(\\n456:             data, precision, floatmode, suppress, sign, legacy=legacy),\\n457:         'longfloat': lambda: FloatingFormat(\\n458:             data, precision, floatmode, suppress, sign, legacy=legacy),\\n459:         'complexfloat': lambda: ComplexFloatingFormat(\\n460:             data, precision, floatmode, suppress, sign, legacy=legacy),\\n461:         'longcomplexfloat': lambda: ComplexFloatingFormat(\\n462:             data, precision, floatmode, suppress, sign, legacy=legacy),\\n463:         'datetime': lambda: DatetimeFormat(data, legacy=legacy),\\n464:         'timedelta': lambda: TimedeltaFormat(data),\\n465:         'object': lambda: _object_format,\\n466:         'void': lambda: str_format,\\n467:         'numpystr': lambda: repr_format}\\n468: \\n469:     # we need to wrap values in `formatter` in a lambda, so that the interface\\n470:     # is the same as the above values.\\n471:     def indirect(x):\\n472:         return lambda: x\\n473: \\n474:     if formatter is not None:\\n475:         fkeys = [k for k in formatter.keys() if formatter[k] is not None]\\n476:         if 'all' in fkeys:\\n477:             for key in formatdict.keys():\\n478:                 formatdict[key] = indirect(formatter['all'])\\n479:         if 'int_kind' in fkeys:\\n480:             for key in ['int']:\\n481:                 formatdict[key] = indirect(formatter['int_kind'])\\n482:         if 'float_kind' in fkeys:\\n483:             for key in ['float', 'longfloat']:\\n484:                 formatdict[key] = indirect(formatter['float_kind'])\\n485:         if 'complex_kind' in fkeys:\\n486:             for key in ['complexfloat', 'longcomplexfloat']:\\n487:                 formatdict[key] = indirect(formatter['complex_kind'])\\n488:         if 'str_kind' in fkeys:\\n489:             formatdict['numpystr'] = indirect(formatter['str_kind'])\\n490:         for key in formatdict.keys():\\n491:             if key in fkeys:\\n492:                 formatdict[key] = indirect(formatter[key])\\n493: \\n494:     return formatdict\\n495: \\n496: def _get_format_function(data, **options):\\n497:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n498:     find the right formatting function for the dtype_\\n499:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n500:     dtype_ = data.dtype\\n501:     dtypeobj = dtype_.type\\n502:     formatdict = _get_formatdict(data, **options)\\n503:     if dtypeobj is None:\\n504:         return formatdict[\\\"numpystr\\\"]()\\n505:     elif issubclass(dtypeobj, _nt.bool):\\n506:         return formatdict['bool']()\\n507:     elif issubclass(dtypeobj, _nt.integer):\\n508:         if issubclass(dtypeobj, _nt.timedelta64):\\n509:             return formatdict['timedelta']()\\n510:         else:\\n511:             return formatdict['int']()\\n512:     elif issubclass(dtypeobj, _nt.floating):\\n513:         if issubclass(dtypeobj, _nt.longdouble):\\n514:             return formatdict['longfloat']()\\n515:         else:\\n516:             return formatdict['float']()\\n517:     elif issubclass(dtypeobj, _nt.complexfloating):\\n518:         if issubclass(dtypeobj, _nt.clongdouble):\\n519:             return formatdict['longcomplexfloat']()\\n520:         else:\\n521:             return formatdict['complexfloat']()\\n522:     elif issubclass(dtypeobj, (_nt.str_, _nt.bytes_)):\\n523:         return formatdict['numpystr']()\\n524:     elif issubclass(dtypeobj, _nt.datetime64):\\n525:         return formatdict['datetime']()\\n526:     elif issubclass(dtypeobj, _nt.object_):\\n527:         return formatdict['object']()\\n528:     elif issubclass(dtypeobj, _nt.void):\\n529:         if dtype_.names is not None:\\n530:             return StructuredVoidFormat.from_data(data, **options)\\n531:         else:\\n532:             return formatdict['void']()\\n533:     else:\\n534:         return formatdict['numpystr']()\\n535: \\n536: \\n537: def _recursive_guard(fillvalue='...'):\\n538:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n539:     Like the python 3.2 reprlib.recursive_repr, but forwards *args and **kwargs\\n540: \\n541:     Decorates a function such that if it calls itself with the same first\\n542:     argument, it returns `fillvalue` instead of recursing.\\n543: \\n544:     Largely copied from reprlib.recursive_repr\\n545:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n546: \\n547:     def decorating_function(f):\\n548:         repr_running = set()\\n549: \\n550:         @functools.wraps(f)\\n551:         def wrapper(self, *args, **kwargs):\\n552:             key = id(self), get_ident()\\n553:             if key in repr_running:\\n554:                 return fillvalue\\n555:             repr_running.add(key)\\n556:             try:\\n557:                 return f(self, *args, **kwargs)\\n558:             finally:\\n559:                 repr_running.discard(key)\\n560: \\n561:         return wrapper\\n562: \\n563:     return decorating_function\\n564: \\n565: \\n566: # gracefully handle recursive calls, when object arrays contain themselves\\n567: @_recursive_guard()\\n568: def _array2string(a, options, separator=' ', prefix=\\\"\\\"):\\n569:     # The formatter __init__s in _get_format_function cannot deal with\\n570:     # subclasses yet, and we also need to avoid recursion issues in\\n571:     # _formatArray with subclasses which return 0d arrays in place of scalars\\n572:     data = asarray(a)\\n573:     if a.shape == ():\\n574:         a = data\\n575: \\n576:     if a.size > options['threshold']:\\n577:         summary_insert = \\\"...\\\"\\n578:         data = _leading_trailing(data, options['edgeitems'])\\n579:     else:\\n580:         summary_insert = \\\"\\\"\\n581: \\n582:     # find the right formatting function for the array\\n583:     format_function = _get_format_function(data, **options)\\n584: \\n585:     # skip over \\\"[\\\"\\n586:     next_line_prefix = \\\" \\\"\\n587:     # skip over array(\\n588:     next_line_prefix += \\\" \\\"*len(prefix)\\n589: \\n590:     lst = _formatArray(a, format_function, options['linewidth'],\\n591:                        next_line_prefix, separator, options['edgeitems'],\\n592:                        summary_insert, options['legacy'])\\n593:     return lst\\n594: \\n595: \\n596: def _array2string_dispatcher(\\n597:         a, max_line_width=None, precision=None,\\n598:         suppress_small=None, separator=None, prefix=None,\\n599:         style=None, formatter=None, threshold=None,\\n600:         edgeitems=None, sign=None, floatmode=None, suffix=None,\\n601:         *, legacy=None):\\n602:     return (a,)\\n603: \\n604: \\n605: @array_function_dispatch(_array2string_dispatcher, module='numpy')\\n606: def array2string(a, max_line_width=None, precision=None,\\n607:                  suppress_small=None, separator=' ', prefix=\\\"\\\",\\n608:                  style=np._NoValue, formatter=None, threshold=None,\\n609:                  edgeitems=None, sign=None, floatmode=None, suffix=\\\"\\\",\\n610:                  *, legacy=None):\\n611:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n612:     Return a string representation of an array.\\n613: \\n614:     Parameters\\n615:     ----------\\n616:     a : ndarray\\n617:         Input array.\\n618:     max_line_width : int, optional\\n619:         Inserts newlines if text is longer than `max_line_width`.\\n620:         Defaults to ``numpy.get_printoptions()['linewidth']``.\\n621:     precision : int or None, optional\\n622:         Floating point precision.\\n623:         Defaults to ``numpy.get_printoptions()['precision']``.\\n624:     suppress_small : bool, optional\\n625:         Represent numbers \\\"very close\\\" to zero as zero; default is False.\\n626:         Very close is defined by precision: if the precision is 8, e.g.,\\n627:         numbers smaller (in absolute value) than 5e-9 are represented as\\n628:         zero.\\n629:         Defaults to ``numpy.get_printoptions()['suppress']``.\\n630:     separator : str, optional\\n631:         Inserted between elements.\\n632:     prefix : str, optional\\n633:     suffix : str, optional\\n634:         The length of the prefix and suffix strings are used to respectively\\n635:         align and wrap the output. An array is typically printed as::\\n636: \\n637:           prefix + array2string(a) + suffix\\n638: \\n639:         The output is left-padded by the length of the prefix string, and\\n640:         wrapping is forced at the column ``max_line_width - len(suffix)``.\\n641:         It should be noted that the content of prefix and suffix strings are\\n642:         not included in the output.\\n643:     style : _NoValue, optional\\n644:         Has no effect, do not use.\\n645: \\n646:         .. deprecated:: 1.14.0\\n647:     formatter : dict of callables, optional\\n648:         If not None, the keys should indicate the type(s) that the respective\\n649:         formatting function applies to.  Callables should return a string.\\n650:         Types that are not specified (by their corresponding keys) are handled\\n651:         by the default formatters.  Individual types for which a formatter\\n652:         can be set are:\\n653: \\n654:         - 'bool'\\n655:         - 'int'\\n656:         - 'timedelta' : a `numpy.timedelta64`\\n657:         - 'datetime' : a `numpy.datetime64`\\n658:         - 'float'\\n659:         - 'longfloat' : 128-bit floats\\n660:         - 'complexfloat'\\n661:         - 'longcomplexfloat' : composed of two 128-bit floats\\n662:         - 'void' : type `numpy.void`\\n663:         - 'numpystr' : types `numpy.bytes_` and `numpy.str_`\\n664: \\n665:         Other keys that can be used to set a group of types at once are:\\n666: \\n667:         - 'all' : sets all types\\n668:         - 'int_kind' : sets 'int'\\n669:         - 'float_kind' : sets 'float' and 'longfloat'\\n670:         - 'complex_kind' : sets 'complexfloat' and 'longcomplexfloat'\\n671:         - 'str_kind' : sets 'numpystr'\\n672:     threshold : int, optional\\n673:         Total number of array elements which trigger summarization\\n674:         rather than full repr.\\n675:         Defaults to ``numpy.get_printoptions()['threshold']``.\\n676:     edgeitems : int, optional\\n677:         Number of array items in summary at beginning and end of\\n678:         each dimension.\\n679:         Defaults to ``numpy.get_printoptions()['edgeitems']``.\\n680:     sign : string, either '-', '+', or ' ', optional\\n681:         Controls printing of the sign of floating-point types. If '+', always\\n682:         print the sign of positive values. If ' ', always prints a space\\n683:         (whitespace character) in the sign position of positive values.  If\\n684:         '-', omit the sign character of positive values.\\n685:         Defaults to ``numpy.get_printoptions()['sign']``.\\n686: \\n687:         .. versionchanged:: 2.0\\n688:              The sign parameter can now be an integer type, previously\\n689:              types were floating-point types.\\n690: \\n691:     floatmode : str, optional\\n692:         Controls the interpretation of the `precision` option for\\n693:         floating-point types.\\n694:         Defaults to ``numpy.get_printoptions()['floatmode']``.\\n695:         Can take the following values:\\n696: \\n697:         - 'fixed': Always print exactly `precision` fractional digits,\\n698:           even if this would print more or fewer digits than\\n699:           necessary to specify the value uniquely.\\n700:         - 'unique': Print the minimum number of fractional digits necessary\\n701:           to represent each value uniquely. Different elements may\\n702:           have a different number of digits.  The value of the\\n703:           `precision` option is ignored.\\n704:         - 'maxprec': Print at most `precision` fractional digits, but if\\n705:           an element can be uniquely represented with fewer digits\\n706:           only print it with that many.\\n707:         - 'maxprec_equal': Print at most `precision` fractional digits,\\n708:           but if every element in the array can be uniquely\\n709:           represented with an equal number of fewer digits, use that\\n710:           many digits for all elements.\\n711:     legacy : string or `False`, optional\\n712:         If set to the string ``'1.13'`` enables 1.13 legacy printing mode. This\\n713:         approximates numpy 1.13 print output by including a space in the sign\\n714:         position of floats and different behavior for 0d arrays. If set to\\n715:         `False`, disables legacy mode. Unrecognized strings will be ignored\\n716:         with a warning for forward compatibility.\\n717: \\n718:     Returns\\n719:     -------\\n720:     array_str : str\\n721:         String representation of the array.\\n722: \\n723:     Raises\\n724:     ------\\n725:     TypeError\\n726:         if a callable in `formatter` does not return a string.\\n727: \\n728:     See Also\\n729:     --------\\n730:     array_str, array_repr, set_printoptions, get_printoptions\\n731: \\n732:     Notes\\n733:     -----\\n734:     If a formatter is specified for a certain type, the `precision` keyword is\\n735:     ignored for that type.\\n736: \\n737:     This is a very flexible function; `array_repr` and `array_str` are using\\n738:     `array2string` internally so keywords with the same name should work\\n739:     identically in all three functions.\\n740: \\n741:     Examples\\n742:     --------\\n743:     >>> import numpy as np\\n744:     >>> x = np.array([1e-16,1,2,3])\\n745:     >>> np.array2string(x, precision=2, separator=',',\\n746:     ...                       suppress_small=True)\\n747:     '[0.,1.,2.,3.]'\\n748: \\n749:     >>> x  = np.arange(3.)\\n750:     >>> np.array2string(x, formatter={'float_kind':lambda x: \\\"%.2f\\\" % x})\\n751:     '[0.00 1.00 2.00]'\\n752: \\n753:     >>> x  = np.arange(3)\\n754:     >>> np.array2string(x, formatter={'int':lambda x: hex(x)})\\n755:     '[0x0 0x1 0x2]'\\n756: \\n757:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n758: \\n759:     overrides = _make_options_dict(precision, threshold, edgeitems,\\n760:                                    max_line_width, suppress_small, None, None,\\n761:                                    sign, formatter, floatmode, legacy)\\n762:     options = format_options.get().copy()\\n763:     options.update(overrides)\\n764: \\n765:     if options['legacy'] <= 113:\\n766:         if style is np._NoValue:\\n767:             style = repr\\n768: \\n769:         if a.shape == () and a.dtype.names is None:\\n770:             return style(a.item())\\n771:     elif style is not np._NoValue:\\n772:         # Deprecation 11-9-2017  v1.14\\n773:         warnings.warn(\\\"'style' argument is deprecated and no longer functional\\\"\\n774:                       \\\" except in 1.13 'legacy' mode\\\",\\n775:                       DeprecationWarning, stacklevel=2)\\n776: \\n777:     if options['legacy'] > 113:\\n778:         options['linewidth'] -= len(suffix)\\n779: \\n780:     # treat as a null array if any of shape elements == 0\\n781:     if a.size == 0:\\n782:         return \\\"[]\\\"\\n783: \\n784:     return _array2string(a, options, separator, prefix)\\n785: \\n786: \\n787: def _extendLine(s, line, word, line_width, next_line_prefix, legacy):\\n788:     needs_wrap = len(line) + len(word) > line_width\\n789:     if legacy > 113:\\n790:         # don't wrap lines if it won't help\\n791:         if len(line) <= len(next_line_prefix):\\n792: \\n800: \\n801: def _extendLine_pretty(s, line, word, line_width, next_line_prefix, legacy):\\n802:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n803:     Extends line with nicely formatted (possibly multi-line) string separator, edge_items, summary_insert, legacy):\\n830:     \\\"\\\"\\\"formatArray is designed for two modes of operation:\\n831: \\n832:     1. Summarized output\\n835: \\n836:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n837:     def recurser(index, hanging_indent, curr_width):\\n838:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n839:         By using this local function, we don't need to recurse with all the\\n840:         arguments. Since this function is not created recursively, the cost is\\n841:         not significant\\n842:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n843:         axis = len(index)\\n844:         axes_left = a.ndim - axis\\n845: \\n846:         if axes_left == 0:\\n847:             return format_function(a[index])\\n848: \\n849:         # when recursing, add a space to align with the [ added, and reduce the\\n850:         # length of the line by 1\\n851:         next_hanging_indent = hanging_indent + ' '\\n852:         if legacy <= 113:\\n853:             next_width = curr_width\\n854:         else:\\n855:             next_width = curr_width - \\n19: \\n20: def _flop_count(idx_contraction, inner, num_terms, size_dictionary):\\n21:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n22:     Computes the number of FLOPS in the contraction.\\n23: \\n24:     Parameters\\n25:     ----------\\n26:     idx_contraction : iterable\\n27:         The indices involved in the contraction\\n28:     inner : bool\\n29:         Does this contraction require an inner product?\\n30:     num_terms : int\\n31:         The number of terms in a contraction\\n32:     size_dictionary : dict\\n33:         The size of each of the indices in idx_contraction\\n34: \\n35:     Returns\\n36:     -------\\n37:     flop_count : int\\n38:         The total number of FLOPS required for the contraction.\\n39: \\n40:     Examples\\n41:     --------\\n42: \\n43:     >>> _flop_count('abc', False, 1, {'a': 2, 'b':3, 'c':5})\\n44:     30\\n45: \\n46:     >>> _flop_count('abc', True, 2, {'a': 2, 'b':3, 'c':5})\\n47:     60\\n48: \\n49:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n50: \\n51:     overall_size = _compute_size_by_dict(idx_contraction, size_dictionary)\\n52:     op_factor = max(1, num_terms - 1)\\n53:     if inner:\\n54:         op_factor += 1\\n55: \\n56:     return overall_size * op_factor\\n57: \\n58: def _compute_size_by_dict(indices, idx_dict):\\n59:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n60:     Computes the product of the elements in indices based on the dictionary\\n61:     idx_dict.\\n62: \\n63:     Parameters\\n64:     ----------\\n65:     indices : iterable\\n66:         Indices to base the product on.\\n67:     idx_dict : dictionary\\n68:         Dictionary of index sizes\\n69: \\n70:     Returns\\n71:     -------\\n72:     ret : int\\n73:         The resulting product.\\n74: \\n75:     Examples\\n76:     --------\\n77:     >>> _compute_size_by_dict('abbc', {'a': 2, 'b':3, 'c':5})\\n78:     90\\n79: \\n80:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n81:     ret = 1\\n82:     for i in indices:\\n83:         ret *= idx_dict[i]\\n84:     return ret\\n85: \\n86: \\n87: def _find_contraction(positions, input_sets, output_set):\\n88:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n89:     Finds the contraction for a given set of input and output sets.\\n90: \\n91:     Parameters\\n92:     ----------\\n93:     positions : iterable\\n94:         Integer positions of terms used in the contraction.\\n95:     input_sets : list\\n96:         List of sets that represent the lhs side of the einsum subscript\\n97:     output_set : set\\n98:         Set that represents the rhs side of the overall einsum subscript\\n99: \\n100:     Returns\\n101:     -------\\n102:     new_result : set\\n103:         The indices of the resulting contraction\\n104:     remaining : list\\n105:         List of sets that have not been contracted, the new set is appended to\\n106:         the end of this list\\n107:     idx_removed : set\\n108:         Indices removed from the entire contraction\\n109:     idx_contraction : set\\n110:         The indices used in the current contraction\\n111: \\n112:     Examples\\n113:     --------\\n114: \\n115:     # A simple dot product test case\\n116:     >>> pos = (0, 1)\\n117:     >>> isets = [set('ab'), set('bc')]\\n118:     >>> oset = set('ac')\\n119:     >>> _find_contraction(pos, isets, oset)\\n120:     ({'a', 'c'}, [{'a', 'c'}], {'b'}, {'a', 'b', 'c'})\\n121: \\n122:     # A more complex case with additional terms in the contraction\\n123:     >>> pos = (0, 2)\\n124:     >>> isets = [set('abd'), set('ac'), set('bdc')]\\n125:     >>> oset = set('ac')\\n126:     >>> _find_contraction(pos, isets, oset)\\n127:     ({'a', 'c'}, [{'a', 'c'}, {'a', 'c'}], {'b', 'd'}, {'a', 'b', 'c', 'd'})\\n128:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n129: \\n130:     idx_contract = set()\\n131:     idx_remain = output_set.copy()\\n132:     remaining = []\\n133:     for ind, value in enumerate(input_sets):\\n134:         if ind in positions:\\n135:             idx_contract |= value\\n136:         else:\\n137:             remaining.append(value)\\n138:             idx_remain |= value\\n139: \\n140:     new_result = idx_remain & idx_contract\\n141:     idx_removed = (idx_contract - new_result)\\n142:     remaining.append(new_result)\\n143: \\n144:     return (new_result, remaining, idx_removed, idx_contract)\\n145: \\n146: \\n147: def _optimal_path(input_sets, output_set, idx_dict, memory_limit):\\n148:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n149:     Computes all possible pair contractions, sieves the results based\\n150:     on ``memory_limit`` and returns the lowest cost path. This algorithm\\n151:     scales factorial with respect to the elements in the list ``input_sets``.\\n152: \\n153:     Parameters\\n154:     ----------\\n155:     input_sets : list\\n156:         List of sets that represent the lhs side of the einsum subscript\\n157:     output_set : set\\n158:         Set that represents the rhs side of the overall einsum subscript\\n159:     idx_dict : dictionary\\n160:         Dictionary of index sizes\\n161:     memory_limit : int\\n162:         The maximum number of elements in a temporary array\\n163: \\n164:     Returns\\n165:     -------\\n166:     path : list\\n167:         The optimal contraction order within the memory limit constraint.\\n168: \\n169:     Examples\\n170:     --------\\n171:     >>> isets = [set('abd'), set('ac'), set('bdc')]\\n172:     >>> oset = set()\\n173:     >>> idx_sizes = {'a': 1, 'b':2, 'c':3, 'd':4}\\n174:     >>> _optimal_path(isets, oset, idx_sizes, 5000)\\n175:     [(0, 2), (0, 1)]\\n176:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n177: \\n178:     full_results = [(0, [], input_sets)]\\n179:     for iteration in range(len(input_sets) - 1):\\n180:         iter_results = []\\n181: \\n182:         # Compute all unique pairs\\n183:         for curr in full_results:\\n184:             cost, positions, remaining = curr\\n185:             for con in itertools.combinations(\\n186:                 range(len(input_sets) - iteration), 2\\n187:             ):\\n188: \\n189:                 # Find the contraction\\n190:                 cont = _find_contraction(con, remaining, output_set)\\n191:                 new_result, new_input_sets, idx_removed, idx_contract = cont\\n192: \\n193:                 # Sieve the results based on memory_limit\\n194:                 new_size = _compute_size_by_dict(new_result, idx_dict)\\n195:                 if new_size > memory_limit:\\n196:                     continue\\n197: \\n198:                 # Build (total_cost, positions, indices_remaining)\\n199:                 total_cost = cost + _flop_count(\\n200:                     idx_contract, idx_removed, len(con), idx_dict\\n201:                 )\\n202:                 new_pos = positions + [con]\\n203:                 iter_results.append((total_cost, new_pos, new_input_sets))\\n204: \\n205:         # Update combinatorial list, if we did not find anything return best\\n206:         # path + remaining contractions\\n207:         if iter_results:\\n208:             full_results = iter_results\\n209:         else:\\n210:             path = min(full_results, key=lambda x: x[0])[1]\\n211:             path += [tuple(range(len(input_sets) - iteration))]\\n212:             return path\\n213: \\n214:     # If we have not found anything return single einsum contraction\\n215:     if len(full_results) == 0:\\n216:         return [tuple(range(len(input_sets)))]\\n217: \\n218:     path = min(full_results, key=lambda x: x[0])[1]\\n219:     return path\\n220: \\n221: def _parse_possible_contraction(\\n222:         positions, input_sets, output_set, idx_dict,\\n223:         memory_limit, path_cost, naive_cost\\n224:     ):\\n225:     \\\"\\\"\\\"Compute the cost (removed size + flops) and resultant indices for\\n226:     performing the contraction specified by ``positions``.\\n227: \\n228:     Parameters\\n229:     ----------\\n230:     positions : tuple of int\\n231:         The locations of the proposed tensors to contract.\\n232:     input_sets : list of sets\\n233:         The indices found on each tensors.\\n234:     output_set : set\\n235:         The output indices of the expression.\\n236:     idx_dict : dict\\n237:         Mapping of each index to its size.\\n238:     memory_limit : int\\n239:         The total allowed size for an intermediary tensor.\\n240:     path_cost : int\\n241:         The contraction cost so far.\\n242:     naive_cost : int\\n243:         The cost of the unoptimized expression.\\n244: \\n245:     Returns\\n246:     -------\\n247:     cost : (int, int)\\n248:         A tuple containing the size of any indices removed, and the flop cost.\\n249:     positions : tuple of int\\n250:         The locations of the proposed tensors to contract.\\n251:     new_input_sets : list of sets\\n252:         The resulting new list of indices if this proposed contraction\\n253:         is performed.\\n254: \\n255:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n256: \\n257:     # Find the contraction\\n258:     contract = _find_contraction(positions, input_sets, output_set)\\n259:     idx_result, new_input_sets, idx_removed, idx_contract = contract\\n260: \\n261:     # Sieve the results based on memory_limit\\n262:     new_size = _compute_size_by_dict(idx_result, idx_dict)\\n263:     if new_size > memory_limit:\\n264:         return None\\n265: \\n266:     # Build sort tuple\\n267:     old_sizes = (\\n268:         _compute_size_by_dict(input_sets[p], idx_dict) for p in positions\\n269:     )\\n270:     removed_size = sum(old_sizes) - new_size\\n271: \\n272:     # NB: removed_size used to be just the size of any removed indices i.e.:\\n273:     #     helpers.compute_size_by_dict(idx_removed, idx_dict)\\n274:     cost = _flop_count(idx_contract, idx_removed, len(positions), idx_dict)\\n275:     sort = (-removed_size, cost)\\n276: \\n277:     # Sieve based on total cost as well\\n278:     if (path_cost + cost) > naive_cost:\\n279:         return None\\n280: \\n281:     # Add contraction to possible choices\\n282:     return [sort, positions, new_input_sets]\\n283: \\n284: \\n285: def _update_other_results(results, best):\\n286:     \\\"\\\"\\\"Update the positions and provisional input_sets of ``results``\\n287:     based on performing the contraction result ``best``. Remove any\\n288:     involving the tensors contracted.\\n289: \\n290:     Parameters\\n291:     ----------\\n292:     results : list\\n293:         List of contraction results produced by\\n294:         ``_parse_possible_contraction``.\\n295:     best : list\\n296:         The best contraction of ``results`` i.e. the one that\\n297:         will be performed.\\n298: \\n299:     Returns\\n300:     -------\\n301:     mod_results : list\\n302:         The list of modified results, updated with outcome of\\n303:         ``best`` contraction.\\n304:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n305: \\n306:     best_con = best[1]\\n307:     bx, by = best_con\\n308:     mod_results = []\\n309: \\n310:     for cost, (x, y), con_sets in results:\\n311: \\n312:         # Ignore results involving tensors just contracted\\n313:         if x in best_con or y in best_con:\\n314:             continue\\n315: \\n316:         # Update the input_sets\\n317:         del con_sets[by - int(by > x) - int(by > y)]\\n318:         del con_sets[bx - int(bx > x) - int(bx > y)]\\n319:         con_sets.insert(-1, best[2][-1])\\n320: \\n321:         # Update the position indices\\n322:         mod_con = x - int(x > bx) - int(x > by), y - int(y > bx) - int(y > by)\\n323:         mod_results.append((cost, mod_con, con_sets))\\n324: \\n325:     return mod_results\\n326: \\n327: def _greedy_path(input_sets, output_set, idx_dict, memory_limit):\\n328:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n329:     Finds the path by contracting the best pair until the input list is\\n330:     exhausted. The best pair is found by minimizing the tuple\\n331:     ``(-prod(indices_removed), cost)``.  What this amounts to is prioritizing\\n332:     matrix multiplication or inner product operations, then Hadamard like\\n333:     operations, and finally outer operations. Outer products are limited by\\n334:     ``memory_limit``. This algorithm scales cubically with respect to the\\n335:     number of elements in the list ``input_sets``.\\n336: \\n337:     Parameters\\n338:     ----------\\n339:     input_sets : list\\n340:         List of sets that represent the lhs side of the einsum subscript\\n341:     output_set : set\\n342:         Set that represents the rhs side of the overall einsum subscript\\n343:     idx_dict : dictionary\\n344:         Dictionary of index sizes\\n345:     memory_limit : int\\n346:         The maximum number of elements in a temporary array\\n347: \\n348:     Returns\\n349:     -------\\n350:     path : list\\n351:         The greedy contraction order within the memory limit constraint.\\n352: \\n353:     Examples\\n354:     --------\\n355:     >>> isets = [set('abd'), set('ac'), set('bdc')]\\n356:     >>> oset = set()\\n357:     >>> idx_sizes = {'a': 1, 'b':2, 'c':3, 'd':4}\\n358:     >>> _greedy_path(isets, oset, idx_sizes, 5000)\\n359:     [(0, 2), (0, 1)]\\n360:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n361: \\n362:     # Handle trivial cases that leaked through\\n363:     if len(input_sets) == 1:\\n364:         return [(0,)]\\n365:     elif len(input_sets) == 2:\\n366:         return [(0, 1)]\\n367: \\n368:     # Build up a naive cost\\n369:     contract = _find_contraction(\\n370:         range(len(input_sets)), input_sets, output_set\\n371:     )\\n372:     idx_result, new_input_sets, idx_removed, idx_contract = contract\\n373:     naive_cost = _flop_count(\\n374:         idx_contract, idx_removed, len(input_sets), idx_dict\\n375:     )\\n376: \\n377:     # Initially iterate over all pairs\\n378:     comb_iter = itertools.combinations(range(len(input_sets)), 2)\\n379:     known_contractions = []\\n380: \\n381:     path_cost = 0\\n382:     path = []\\n383: \\n384:     for iteration in range(len(input_sets) - 1):\\n385: \\n386:         # Iterate over all pairs on the first step, only previously\\n387:         # found pairs on subsequent steps\\n388:         for positions in comb_iter:\\n389: \\n390:             # Always initially ignore outer products\\n391:             if input_sets[positions[0]].isdisjoint(input_sets[positions[1]]):\\n392:                 continue\\n393: \\n394:             result = _parse_possible_contraction(\\n395:                 positions, input_sets, output_set, idx_dict,\\n396:                 memory_limit, path_cost, naive_cost\\n397:             )\\n398:             if result is not None:\\n399:                 known_contractions.append(result)\\n400: \\n401:         # If we do not have a inner contraction, rescan pairs\\n402:         # including outer products\\n403:         if len(known_contractions) == 0:\\n404: \\n405:             # Then check the outer products\\n406:             for positions in itertools.combinations(\\n407:                 range(len(input_sets)), 2\\n408:             ):\\n409:                 result = _parse_possible_contraction(\\n410:                     positions, input_sets, output_set, idx_dict,\\n411:                     memory_limit, path_cost, naive_cost\\n412:                 )\\n413:                 if result is not None:\\n414:                     known_contractions.append(result)\\n415: \\n416:             # If we still did not find any remaining contractions,\\n417:             # default back to einsum like behavior\\n418:             if len(known_contractions) == 0:\\n419:                 path.append(tuple(range(len(input_sets))))\\n420:                 break\\n421: \\n422:         # Sort based on first index\\n423:         best = min(known_contractions, key=lambda x: x[0])\\n424: \\n425:         # Now propagate as many unused contractions as possible\\n426:         # to the next iteration\\n427:         known_contractions = _update_other_results(known_contractions, best)\\n428: \\n429:         # Next iteration only compute contractions with the new tensor\\n430:         # All other contractions have been accounted for\\n431:         input_sets = best[2]\\n432:         new_tensor_pos = len(input_sets) - 1\\n433:         comb_iter = ((i, new_tensor_pos) for i in range(new_tensor_pos))\\n434: \\n435:         # Update path and total cost\\n436:         path.append(best[1])\\n437:         path_cost += best[0][1]\\n438: \\n439:     return path\\n440: \\n441: \\n442: def _can_dot(inputs, result, idx_removed):\\n443:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n444:     Checks if we can use BLAS (np.tensordot) call and its beneficial to do so.\\n445: \\n446:     Parameters\\n447:     ----------\\n448:     inputs : list of str\\n449:         Specifies the subscripts for summation.\\n450:     result : str\\n451:         Resulting summation.\\n452:     idx_removed : set\\n453:         Indices that are removed in the summation\\n454: \\n455: \\n456:     Returns\\n457:     -------\\n458:     type : bool\\n459:         Returns true if BLAS should and can be used, else False\\n460: \\n461:     Notes\\n462:     -----\\n463:     If the operations is BLAS level 1 or 2 and is not already aligned\\n464:     we default back to einsum as the memory movement to copy is more\\n465:     costly than the operation itself.\\n466: \\n467: \\n468:     Examples\\n469:     --------\\n470: \\n471:     # Standard GEMM operation\\n472:     >>> _can_dot(['ij', 'jk'], 'ik', set('j'))\\n473:     True\\n474: \\n475:     # Can use the standard BLAS, but requires odd data movement\\n476:     >>> _can_dot(['ijj', 'jk'], 'ik', set('j'))\\n477:     False\\n478: \\n479:     # DDOT where the memory is not aligned\\n480:     >>> _can_dot(['ijk', 'ikj'], '', set('ijk'))\\n481:     False\\n482: \\n483:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n484: \\n485:     # All `dot` calls remove indices\\n486:     if len(idx_removed) == 0:\\n487:         return False\\n488: \\n489:     # BLAS can only handle two operands\\n490:     if len(inputs) != 2:\\n491:         return False\\n492: \\n493:     input_left, input_right = inputs\\n494: \\n495:     for c in set(input_left + input_right):\\n496:         # can't deal with repeated indices on same input or more than 2 total\\n497:         nl, nr = input_left.count(c), input_right.count(c)\\n498:         if (nl > 1) or (nr > 1) or (nl + nr > 2):\\n499:             return False\\n500: \\n501:         # can't do implicit summation or dimension collapse e.g.\\n502:         #     \\\"ab,bc->c\\\" (implicitly sum over 'a')\\n503:         #     \\\"ab,ca->ca\\\" (take diagonal of 'a')\\n504:         if nl + nr - 1 == int(c in result):\\n505:             return False\\n506: \\n507:     # Build a few temporaries\\n508:     set_left = set(input_left)\\n509:     set_right = set(input_right)\\n510:     keep_left = set_left - idx_removed\\n511:     keep_right = set_right - idx_removed\\n512:     rs = len(idx_removed)\\n513: \\n514:     # At this point we are a DOT, GEMV, or GEMM operation\\n515: \\n516:     # Handle inner products\\n517: \\n518:     # DDOT with aligned data\\n519:     if input_left == input_right:\\n520:         return True\\n521: \\n522:     # DDOT without aligned data (better to use einsum)\\n523:     if set_left == set_right:\\n524:         return False\\n525: \\n526:     # Handle the 4 possible (aligned) GEMV or GEMM cases\\n527: \\n528:     # GEMM or GEMV no transpose\\n529:     if input_left[-rs:] == input_right[:rs]:\\n530:         return True\\n531: \\n532:     # GEMM or GEMV transpose both\\n533:     if input_left[:rs] == input_right[-rs:]:\\n534:         return True\\n535: \\n536:     # GEMM or GEMV transpose right\\n537:     if input_left[-rs:] == input_right[-rs:]:\\n538:         return True\\n539: \\n540:     # GEMM or GEMV transpose left\\n541:     if input_left[:rs] == input_right[:rs]:\\n542:         return True\\n543: \\n544:     # Einsum is faster than GEMV if we have to copy data\\n545:     if not keep_left or not keep_right:\\n546:         return False\\n547: \\n548:     # We are a matrix-matrix product, but we need to copy data\\n549:     return True\\n550: \\n551: \\n552: def _parse_einsum_input(operands):\\n553:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n554:     A reproduction of einsum c side einsum parsing in python.\\n555: \\n556:     Returns\\n557:     -------\\n558:     input_strings : contraction\\n564: \\n565:     Examples\\n566:     --------\\n567:     The operand list is simplified to reduce printing:\\n568: \\n569:     >>> np.random.seed(123)\\n570:     >>> a =        raise ValueError(\\\"Subscripts can only contain one '->'.\\\")\\n640: \\n641:     # Parse ellipses\\n642:     if \\\".\\\" in subscripts:\\n643:         used = subscripts.replace(\\\".\\\", \\\"\\\")\\n708:         output_subscript = \\\"\\\"\\n709:         for s in sorted(set(tmp_subscripts)):\\n710:             if s not in einsum_symbols:\\n711:                 raise ValueError(\\\"Character %s is not a valid symbol.\\\" % s)\\n712:             if tmp_subscripts.count(s) == 1:\\n713:                 output_subscript += s\\n714: \\n715:     # Make sure output subscripts are in the input\\n716:     for char in output_subscript:\\n717:         if output_subscript.count(char) != 1:\\n718:             raise ValueError(\\\"Output character %s appeared more than once in \\\"\\n719:                              \\\"the output.\\\" % char)\\n720:         if char not in input_subscripts:\\n721:             raise ValueError(\\\"Output character %s did not appear in the input\\\"\\n722:                              % char)\\n723: \\n724:     # Make sure number operands is equivalent to the number of terms\\n725:     if len(input_subscripts.split(',')) != len(operands):\\n726:         raise ValueError(\\\"Number of einsum subscripts must be equal to the \\\"\\n727:                          \\\"number of operands.\\\")\\n728: \\n729:     return (input_subscripts, output_subscript, operands)\\n730: \\n731: \\n732: def _einsum_path_dispatcher(*operands, optimize=None, einsum_call=None):\\n733:     # NOTE: technically, we should only dispatch on array-like arguments, not\\n734:     # subscripts (given as strings). But separating operands into\\n735:     # arrays/subscripts is a little tricky/slow (given einsum's two supported\\n736:     # signatures), so as a practical shortcut we dispatch on everything.\\n737:     # Strings will be ignored for dispatching since they don't define\\n738:     # __array_function__.\\n739:     return operands\\n740: \\n741: \\n742: @array_function_dispatch(_einsum_path_dispatcher, module='numpy')\\n743: def einsum_path(*operands, optimize='greedy', einsum_call=False):\\n744:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n745:     einsum_path(subscripts, *operands, optimize='greedy')\\n746: \\n747:     Evaluates the lowest cost contraction order for an einsum expression by\\n748:     considering the creation of intermediate arrays.\\n749: \\n750:     Parameters\\n751:     ----------\\n752:     subscripts : str\\n753:         Specifies the subscripts for summation.\\n754:     *operands : list of array_like\\n755:         These are the arrays for the operation.\\n756:     optimize : {bool, list, tuple, 'greedy', 'optimal'}\\n757:         Choose the type of path. If a tuple is provided, the second argument is\\n758:         assumed to be the maximum intermediate size created. If only a single\\n759:         argument is provided the largest input or output array size is used\\n760:         as a maximum intermediate size.\\n761: \\n762:         * if a list is given that starts with ``einsum_path``, uses this as the\\n763:           contraction path\\n764:         * if False no optimization is taken\\n765:         * if True defaults to the 'greedy' algorithm\\n766:         * 'optimal' An algorithm that combinatorially explores all possible\\n767:           ways of contracting the listed tensors and chooses the least costly\\n768:           path. Scales exponentially with the number of terms in the\\n769:           contraction.\\n770:         * 'greedy' An algorithm that chooses the best pair contraction\\n771:           at each step. Effectively, this algorithm searches the largest inner,\\n772:           Hadamard, and then outer products at each step. Scales cubically with\\n773:           the number of terms in the contraction. Equivalent to the 'optimal'\\n774:           path for most contractions.\\n775: \\n776:         Default is 'greedy'.\\n777: \\n778:     Returns\\n779:     -------\\n780:     path : list of tuples\\n781:         A list representation of the einsum path.\\n782:     string_repr : str\\n783:         A printable representation of the einsum path.\\n784: \\n785:     Notes\\n786:     -----\\n787:     The resulting path indicates which terms of the input contraction should be\\n788:     contracted first, the result of this contraction is then appended to the\\n789:     end of the contraction list. This list can then be iterated over until all\\n790:     intermediate contractions are complete.\\n791: \\n792:     See Also\\n793:     --------\\n794:     einsum, linalg.multi_dot\\n795: \\n796:     Examples\\n797:     --------\\n798: \\n799:     We can begin with a chain dot example. In this case, it is optimal to\\n800:     contract the ``b`` and ``c`` tensors first as represented by the first\\n801:     element of the path ``(1, 2)``. The resulting tensor is added to the end\\n802:     of the contraction and the remaining contraction ``(0, 1)`` is then\\n803:     completed.\\n804: \\n805:     >>> np.random.seed(123)\\n806:     >>> a = np.random.rand(2, 2)\\n807:     >>> b = np.random.rand(2, 5)\\n808:     >>> c = np.random.rand(5, 2)\\n809:     >>> path_info = np.einsum_path('ij,jk,kl->il', a, b, c, optimize='greedy')\\n810:     >>> print(path_info[0])\\n811:     ['einsum_path', (1, 2), (0, 1)]\\n812:     >>> print(path_info[1])\\n813:       Complete contraction:  ij,jk,kl->il # may vary\\n814:              Naive scaling:  4\\n815:          Optimized scaling:  3\\n816:           Naive FLOP count:  1.600e+02\\n817:       Optimized FLOP count:  5.600e+01\\n818:        Theoretical speedup:  2.857\\n819:       Largest intermediate:  4.000e+00 elements\\n820:     -------------------------------------------------------------------------\\n821:     scaling                  current                                remaining\\n822:     -------------------------------------------------------------------------\\n823:        3                   kl,jk->jl                                ij,jl->il\\n824:        3                   jl,ij->il                                   il->il\\n825: \\n826: \\n827:     A more complex index transformation example.\\n828: \\n829:     >>> I = np.random.rand(10, 10, 10, 10)\\n830:     >>> C = np.random.rand(10, 10)\\n831:     >>> path_info = np.einsum_path('ea,fb,abcd,gc,hd->efgh', C, C, I, C, C,\\n832:     ...                            optimize='greedy')\\n833: \\n834:     >>> print(path_info[0])\\n835:     ['einsum_path', (0, 2), (0, 3), (0, 2), (0, 1)]\\n836:     >>> print(path_info[1])\\n837:       Complete contraction:  ea,fb,abcd,gc,hd->efgh # may vary\\n838:              Naive scaling:  8\\n839:          Optimized scaling:  5\\n840:           Naive FLOP count:  8.000e+08\\n841:       Optimized FLOP count:  8.000e+05\\n842:        Theoretical speedup:  1000.000\\n843:       Largest intermediate:  1.000e+04 elements\\n844:     --------------------------------------------------------------------------\\n845:     scaling                  current                                remaining\\n846:     --------------------------------------------------------------------------\\n847:        5               abcd,ea->bcde                      fb,gc,hd,bcde->efgh\\n848:        5               bcde,fb->cdef                         gc,hd,cdef->efgh\\n849:        5               cdef,gc->defg                            hd,defg->efgh\\n850:        5               defg,hd->efgh                               efgh->efgh\\n851:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n852: \\n853:     # Figure out what the path really is\\n854:     path_type = optimize\\n855:     if path_type is True:\\n856:         path_type = 'greedy'\\n857:     if path_type is None:\\n858:         path_type = False\\n859: \\n860:     explicit_einsum_path = False\\n861:     memory_limit = None\\n862: \\n863:     # No optimization or a named path algorithm\\n864:     if (path_type is False) or isinstance(path_type, str):\\n865:         pass\\n866: \\n867:     # Given an explicit path\\n868:     elif len(path_type) and (path_type[0] == 'einsum_path'):\\n869:         explicit_einsum_path = True\\n870: \\n871:     # Path tuple with memory limit\\n872:     elif ((len(path_type) == 2) and isinstance(path_type[0], str) and\\n873:             isinstance(path_type[1], (int, float))):\\n874:         memory_limit = int(path_type[1])\\n875:         path_type = path_type[0]\\n876: \\n877:     else:\\n878:         raise TypeError(\\\"Did not understand the path: %s\\\" % str(path_type))\\n879: \\n880:     # Hidden option, only einsum should call this\\n881:     einsum_call_arg = einsum_call\\n882: \\n883:     # Python side parsing\\n884:     input_subscripts, output_subscript, operands = (\\n885:         _parse_einsum_input(operands)\\n886:     )\\n887: \\n888:     # Build a few useful list and sets\\n889:     input_list = input_subscripts.split(',')\\n890:     input_sets = [set(x) for x in input_list]\\n891:     output_set = set(output_subscript)\\n892:     indices = set(input_subscripts.replace(',', ''))\\n893: \\n894:     # Get length of each unique dimension and ensure all dimensions are correct\\n895:     dimension_dict = {}\\n896:     broadcast_indices = [[] for x in range(len(input_list))]\\n897:     for tnum, term in enumerate(input_list):\\n898:         sh = operands[tnum].shape\\n899:         if len(sh) != len(term):\\n900:             raise ValueError(\\\"Einstein sum subscript %s does not contain the \\\"\\n901:                              \\\"correct number of indices for operand %d.\\\"\\n902:                              % (input_subscripts[tnum], tnum))\\n903:         for cnum, char in enumerate(term):\\n904:             dim = sh[cnum]\\n905: \\n906:             # Build out broadcast indices\\n907:             if dim == 1:\\n908:                 broadcast_indices[tnum].append(char)\\n909: \\n910:             if char in dimension_dict.keys():\\n911:                 # For broadcasting cases we always want the largest dim size\\n912:                 if dimension_dict[char] == 1:\\n913:                     dimension_dict[char] = dim\\n914:                 elif dim not in (1, dimension_dict[char]):\\n915:                     raise ValueError(\\\"Size of label '%s' for operand %d (%d) \\\"\\n916:                                      \\\"does not match previous terms (%d).\\\"\\n917:                                      % (char, tnum, dimension_dict[char], dim))\\n918:             else:\\n919:                 dimension_dict[char] = dim\\n920: \\n921:     # Convert broadcast inds to sets\\n922:     broadcast_indices = [set(x) for x in broadcast_indices]\\n923: \\n924:     # Compute size of each input array plus the output array\\n925:     size_list = [_compute_size_by_dict(term, dimension_dict)\\n926:                  for term in input_list + [output_subscript]]\\n927:     max_size = max(size_list)\\n928: \\n929:     if memory_limit is None:\\n930:         memory_arg = max_size\\n931:     else:\\n932:         memory_arg = memory_limit\\n933: \\n934:     # Compute naive cost\\n935:     # This isn't quite right, need to look into exactly how einsum does this\\n936:     inner_product = (sum(len(x) for x in input_sets) - len(indices)) > 0\\n937:     naive_cost = _flop_count(\\n938:         indices, inner_product, len(input_list), dimension_dict\\n939:     )\\n940: \\n941:     # Compute the path\\n942:     if explicit_einsum_path:\\n943:         path = path_type[1:]\\n944:     elif (\\n945:         (path_type is False)\\n946:         or (len(input_list) in [1, 2])\\n947:         or (indices == output_set)\\n948:     ):\\n949:         # Nothing to be optimized, leave it to einsum\\n950:         path = [tuple(range(len(input_list)))]\\n951:     elif path_type == \\\"greedy\\\":\\n952:         path = _greedy_path(\\n953:             input_sets, output_set, dimension_dict, memory_arg\\n954:         )\\n955:     elif path_type == \\\"optimal\\\":\\n956:         path = _optimal_path(\\n957:             input_sets, output_set, dimension_dict, memory_arg\\n958:         )\\n959:     else:\\n960:         raise KeyError(\\\"Path name %s not found\\\", path_type)\\n961: \\n962:     cost_list, scale_list, size_list, contraction_list = [], [], [], []\\n963: \\n964:     # @array_function_dispatch(_rot90_dispatcher)\\n154: def rot90(m, k=1, axes=(0, 1)):\\n155:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n156:     Rotate an array by 90 degrees in the plane specified by axes.\\n157: \\n158:     Rotation direction is from the first towards the second axis.\\n159:     This means for a 2D array with the default `k` and `axes`, the\\n160:     rotation will be counterclockwise.\\n161: \\n162:     Parameters\\n163:     ----------\\n164:     m : array_like\\n165:         Array of two or more dimensions.\\n166:     k : integer\\n167:         Number of times the array is rotated by 90 degrees.\\n168:     axes : (2,) array_like\\n169:         The array is rotated in the plane defined by the axes.\\n170:         Axes must be different.\\n171: \\n172:     Returns\\n173:     -------\\n174:     y : ndarray\\n175:         A rotated view of `m`.\\n176: \\n177:     See Also\\n178:     --------\\n179:     flip : Reverse the order of elements in an array along the given axis.\\n180:     fliplr : Flip an array horizontally.\\n181:     flipud : Flip an array vertically.\\n182: \\n183:     Notes\\n184:     -----\\n185:     ``rot90(m, k=1, axes=(1,0))``  is the reverse of\\n186:     ``rot90(m, k=1, axes=(0,1))``\\n187: \\n188:     ``rot90(m, k=1, axes=(1,0))`` is equivalent to\\n189:     ``rot90(m, k=-1, axes=(0,1))``\\n190: \\n191:     Examples\\n192: @array_function_dispatch(_flip_dispatcher)\\n249: def flip(m, axis=None):\\n250:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n251:     Reverse the order of elements in an array along the given axis.\\n252: \\n253:     The shape of the array is preserved, but the elements are reordered.\\n254: \\n255:     Parameters\\n256:     ----------\\n257:     m : array_like\\n258:         Input array.\\n259:     axis : None or int or tuple of ints, optional\\n260:          Axis or axes along which to flip over. The default,\\n261:          axis=None, will flip over all of the axes of the input array.\\n262:          If axis is negative it counts from the last to the first axis.\\n263: \\n264:          If axis is a tuple of ints, flipping is performed on all of the axes\\n265:          specified in the tuple.\\n266: \\n267:     Returns\\n268:     -------\\n269:     out : array_like\\n270:         A view of `m` with the entries of axis reversed.  Since a view is\\n271:         returned, this operation is done in constant time.\\n272: \\n273:     See Also\\n274:     --------\\n275:     flipud : Flip an array vertically (axis=0).\\n276:     fliplr : Flip an array horizontally (axis=1).\\n277: \\n278:     Notes\\n279:     -----\\n280:     flip(m, 0) is equivalent to flipud(m).\\n281: \\n282:     flip(m, 1) is equivalent to fliplr(m).\\n283: \\n284:     flip(m, n) corresponds to ``m[...,::-1,...]`` with ``::-1`` at position n.\\n285: \\n286:     flip(m) corresponds to ``m[::-1,::-1,...,::-1]`` with ``::-1`` at all\\n287:     positions.\\n288: \\n289:     flip(m, (0, 1)) corresponds to ``m[::-1,::-1,...]`` with ``::-1`` at\\n290:     position 0 and position 1.\\n291: \\n292:     Examples\\n293:     --------\\n294:     >>> import numpy as np\\n295:     >>> A = np.arange(8).reshape((2,2,2))\\n296:     >>> A\\n297:     array([[[0, 1],\\n298:             [2, 3]],\\n299:            [[4, 5],\\n300:             [6, \\n339: @set_module('numpy')\\n340: def iterable(y):\\n341:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n342:     Check whether or not an object can be iterated over.\\n343: \\n344:     Parameters\\n345:     ----------\\n346: np.iterable(2)\\n362:     False\\n363: \\n364:     Notes\\n365:     -----\\n366:     In most cases, the results of ``np.iterable(obj)`` are consistent with\\n367:     ``isinstance(obj, collections.abc.Iterable)``. One notable exception is\\n368:     the treatment of 0-dimensional arrays::\\n369: \\n370:         >>> from collections.abc import Iterable\\n371:         >>> a = np.array(1.0)  # 0-dimensional numpy array\\n372:         >>> isinstance(a, Iterable)\\n373:         True\\n374:         >>> np.iterable(a)\\n375:         False\\n376: \\n377:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n378:     try:\\n379:         iter(y)\\n380:     except TypeError:\\n381:         return False\\n382:     return True\\n383: \\n384: \\n385: def _weights_are_valid(weights, a, axis):\\n386:     \\\"\\\"\\\"Validate weights array.\\n387: \\n388:     We assume, weights is not None.\\n389:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n390:     wgt = np.asanyarray(weights)\\n391: \\n392:     # If `a` is not an array, a\\n425:         conversion is attempted.\\n426:     axis : None or int or tuple of ints, optional\\n427:         Axis or axes along which to average `a`.  The default,\\n428:         `axis=None`, will average over all of the elements of the input array.\\n429:         If axis is negative it counts from the last to the first axis.\\n430:         If axis is a tuple of ints, averaging is performed on all of the axes\\n431:         specified in the tuple instead of a single axis or all the axes as\\n432:         before.\\n433:     weights : array_like, optional\\n434:         An array of weights associated with the values in `a`. Each value in\\n435:         `a` contributes to the average according to its associated weight.\\n436:         The array of weights must be the same shape as `a` if no axis is\\n437:         specified, otherwise the weights must have dimensions and shape\\n438:         consistent with `a` along the specified axis.\\n439:         If `weights=None`, then all data in `a` are assumed to have a\\n440:         weight equal to one.\\n441:         The calculation is::\\n442: \\n443:             avg = sum(a * weights) / sum(weights)\\n444: \\n445:         where the sum is over all included elements.\\n446:         The only constraint on the values of `weights` is that `sum(weights)`\\n447:         must not be 0.\\n448:     returned : bool, optional\\n449:         Default is `False`. If `True`, the tuple (`average`, `sum_of_weights`)\\n450:         is returned, otherwise only the average is returned.\\n451:         If `weights=None`, `sum_of_weights` is equivalent to the number of\\n452:         elements over which the average is taken.\\n453:     keepdims : bool, optional\\n454:         If this is set to True, the axes which are reduced are left\\n455:         in the result as dimensions with size one. With this option,\\n456:         the result will broadcast correctly against the original `a`.\\n457:         *Note:* `keepdims` will not work with instances of `numpy.matrix`\\n458:         or other classes whose methods do not support `keepdims`.\\n459: \\n460:         .. versionadded:: 1.23.0\\n461: \\n462:     Returns\\n463:     -------\\n464:     retval, [sum_of_weights] : array_type or double\\n465:         Return the average along the specified axis. When `returned` is `True`,\\n466:         return a tuple with the average as the first element and the sum\\n467:         of the weights as the second element. `sum_of_weights` is of the\\n468:         same type as `retval`. The result dtype follows a general pattern.\\n469:         If `weights` is None, the result dtype will be that of `a` , or ``float64``\\n470:         if `a` is integral. Otherwise, if `weights` is not None and `a` is non-\\n471:         integral, the result type will be the type of lowest precision capable of\\n472:         representing values of both `a` and `weights`. If `a` happens to be\\n473:         integral, the previous rules still applies but the result dtype will\\n474:         at least be ``float64``.\\n475: \\n476:     Raises\\n477:     ------\\n478:     ZeroDivisionError\\n479:         When all weights along axis are zero. See `numpy.ma.average` for a\\n480:         version robust to this type of error.\\n481:     TypeError\\n482:         When `weights` does not have the same shape as `a`, and `axis=None`.\\n483:     ValueError\\n484:         When `weights` does not have dimensions and shape consistent with `a`\\n485:         along specified `axis`.\\n486: \\n487:     See Also\\n488:     --------\\n489:     mean\\n490: \\n491:     ma.average : average for masked arrays -- useful if your data contains\\n492:                  \\\"missing\\\" values\\n493:     numpy.result_type : Returns the type that results from applying the\\n494:                         numpy type promotion rules to the arguments.\\n495: \\n496:     Examples\\n497:     --------\\n498:     >>> import numpy as np\\n499:     >>> 'A' and 'K' depend on the order of input array a.\\n593:         'C' row-major (C-style),\\n594:         'F' column-major (Fortran-style) memory representation.\\n595:         'A' (any) means 'F' if `a` is Fortran contiguous, 'C' otherwise\\n596:         'K' (keep) preserve input order\\n597:         Defaults to 'C'.\\n598: \\n599:     Returns\\n600:     -------\\n601:     out : ndarray\\n602:         Array interpretation of `a`. Wherever\\n672:         `condlist[i]` is True, `funclist[i](x)` is used as the output value.\\n673: \\n674:         Each boolean array in `condlist` selects a piece of `x`,\\n675:         and should therefore be of the same shape as `x`.\\n676: \\n677:         The length of `condlist` must correspond to that of `funclist`.\\n678:         If one extra function is given, i.e. if\\n679:         ``len(funclist) == len(condlist) + 1``, then that extra function\\n680:         is the default value, used wherever all conditions are false.\\n681:     funclist : list of callables, f(x,*args,**kw), or scalars\\n682:         Each function is evaluated over `x` wherever its corresponding\\n683:         condition is True.  It should take a 1d array as input and give an 1d\\n684:         array or a scalar value as output.  If, instead of a callable,\\n685:         a scalar is provided then a constant function (``lambda x: scalar``) is\\n686:         assumed.\\n687:     args : tuple, optional\\n688:         Any further arguments given to `piecewise` are passed to the functions\\n689:         upon execution, i.e., if called ``piecewise(..., ..., 1, 'a')``, then\\n690:         each function is called as ``f(x, 1, 'a')``.\\n691:     kw : dict, optional\\n692:         Keyword arguments used in calling `piecewise` are passed to the\\n693:         functions upon execution, i.e., if called\\n694:         ``piecewise(..., ..., alpha=1)``, then each function is called as\\n695:         ``f(x, alpha=1)``.\\n696: \\n697:     Returns\\n698:     -------\\n699:     out : ndarray\\n700:         The output is the same shape and type as x and is found by\\n701:         calling the functions in `funclist` on the appropriate portions of `x`,\\n702:         as defined by the boolean arrays in `condlist`.  Portions not covered\\n703:         by any condition have a default value of 0.\\n704: \\n705: \\n706:     See Also\\n707:     --------\\n708:     choose, select, where\\n709: \\n710:     Notes\\n711:     -----\\n712:     This is similar to choose or select, except that functions are\\n713:     evaluated on elements of `x` that satisfy the corresponding condition from\\n714:     `condlist`.\\n715: \\n716:     The result is::\\n717: \\n718:             |--\\n719:             |funclist[0](x[condlist[0]])\\n720:       out = |funclist[1](x[condlist[1]])\\n721:             |...\\n722:             |funclist[n2](x[condlist[n2]])\\n723:             |--\\n724: \\n725:     Examples\\n726:     --------\\n727:     >>> import numpy as np\\n728: \\n729:     Define the signum function, which is -1 for ``x < 0`` and +1 for ``x >= 0``.\\n730: \\n731:     >>> x = np.linspace(-2.5, 2.5, 6)\\n732:     >>> np.piecewise(x, [x < 0, x >= 0], [-1, 1])\\n733:     array([-1., -1., -1.,  1.,  1.,  1.])\\n734: \\n735:     Define the absolute value, which is ``-x`` for ``x <0`` and ``x`` for\\n736:     ``x >= 0``.\\n737: \\n738:     >>> np.piecewise(x, [x < 0, x >= 0], [lambda x: -x, lambda x: x])\\n739:     array([2.5,  1.5,  0.5,  0.5,  1.5,  2.5])\\n740: \\n741:     Apply the same function to a scalar value.\\n742: \\n743:     >>> y = -2\\n744:     >>> np.piecewise(y, [y < 0, y >= 0], [lambda x: -x, lambda x: x])\\n745:     array(2)\\n746: \\n747:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n748:     x = asanyarray(x)\\n749:     n2 = len(funclist)\\n750: \\n751:     # undocumented: single condition is promoted to a list of one condition\\n752:     if isscalar(condlist) or (\\n753:             not isinstance(condlist[0], (list, ndarray)) and x.ndim != 0):\\n754:         condlist = [condlist]\\n755: \\n756:     condlist = asarray(condlist, dtype=bool)\\n757:     n = len(condlist)\\n758: \\n759:     if n == n2 - 'C' means C-order,\\n909:         'F' means F-order, 'A' means 'F' if `a` is Fortran contiguous,\\n910:         'C' otherwise. 'K' means match the layout of `a` as closely\\n911:         as possible. (Note that this function and :meth:`ndarray.copy` are very\\n912:         similar, but have different default values for their order=\\n913:         arguments.)\\n914:     subok : bool, optional\\n915:         If True, then sub-classes will be passed-through, otherwise the\\n916:         returned array will be forced to be a base-class array (defaults to False).\\n917: \\n918:     Returns\\n919:     -------\\n920:     arr : ndarray\\n921:         Array interpretation of `a`.\\n922: \\n923:     See Also\\n924:     --------\\n925:     ndarray.copy : Preferred method for creating an array copy\\n926: \\n927:     Notes\\n928:     -----\\n929:     This is equivalent to:\\n930: \\n931:     >>> np.array(a, copy=True)  #doctest: +SKIP\\n932: \\n933:     The copy made of the data is shallow, i.e., for arrays with object dtype,\\n934:     the new array will point to the same objects.\\n935:     See Examples from `ndarray.copy`.\\n936: \\n937:     Examples\\n938:     --------\\n939:     >>> import numpy as np\\n940: \\n941:     Create an array x, with a reference y and a copy z:\\n942: \\n943:     >>> x = np.array([1, 2, 3])\\n944:     >>> y = x\\n945:     >>> z = np.copy(x)\\n946: \\n947:     Note that, when we modify x, y changes, but not z:\\n948: \\n949:     >>> x[0] = 10\\n950:     >>> x[0] == y[0]\\n951:     True\\n952:     >>> x[0] == z[0]\\n953:     False\\n954: \\n955:     Note that, np.copy clears previously set WRITEABLE=False flag.\\n956: \\n957:     >>> a = np.array([1, 2, 3])\\n958:     >>> a.flags[\\\"WRITEABLE\\\"] = False\\n959:     >>> b = np.copy(a)\\n960:     >>> b.flags[\\\"WRITEABLE\\\"]\\n961:     True\\n962:     >>> b[0] = 3\\n963:     >>> b\\n964:     array([3, 2, 3])\\n965:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n966:     return array(a, order=order, subok=subok, copy=True)\\n967: \\n968: #
