will be interpreted as boolean masks for the\\n53:         corresponding dimension (equivalent to passing in\\n54:         ``np.nonzero(boolean_sequence)``).\\n55: \\n56:     Returns\\n57:     -------\\n58:     out : tuple of ndarrays\\n59:         N arrays with N dimensions each, with N the number of input\\n60:         sequences. Together these arrays form an open mesh.\\n61: \\n62:     See Also\\n63:     --------\\n64:     ogrid, mgrid, meshgrid\\n65: \\n66:     Examples\\n67:     --------\\n68:     >>> import numpy as np\\n69:     >>> a = np.arange(10).reshape(2, 5)\\n70:     >>> a\\n71:     array([[0, 1, 2, 3, 4],\\n72:            [5, 6, 7, 8, 9]])\\n73:     >>> ixgrid = np.ix_([0, 1], [2, 4])\\n74:     >>> ixgrid\\n75:     (array([[0],\\n76:            [1]]), array([[2, 4]]))\\n77:     >>> ixgrid[0].shape, ixgrid[1].shape\\n78:     ((2, 1), (1, 2))\\n79:     >>> a[ixgrid]\\n80:     array([[2, 4],\\n81:            [7, 9]])\\n82: \\n83:     >>> ixgrid = np.ix_([True, True], [2, 4])\\n84:     >>> a[ixgrid]\\n85:     array([[2, 4],\\n86:            [7, 9]])\\n87:     >>> ixgrid = np.ix_([True, True], [False, False, True, False, True])\\n88:     >>> a[ixgrid]\\n89:     array([[2, 4],\\n90:            [7, 9]])\\n91: \\n92:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n93:     out = []\\n94:     nd = len(args)\\n95:     for k, new in enumerate(args):\\n96:         if not isinstance(new, _nx.ndarray):\\n97:             new = np.asarray(new)\\n98:             if new.size == 0:\\n99:                 # Explicitly type empty arrays to avoid float default\\n100:                 new = new.astype(_nx.intp)\\n101:         if new.ndim != 1:\\n102:             raise ValueError(\\\"Cross index must be 1 dimensional\\\")\\n103:         if issubdtype(new.dtype, _nx.bool):\\n104:             new, = new.nonzero()\\n105:         new = new.reshape((1,)*k + (new.size,) + (1,)*(nd-k-1))\\n106:         out.append(new)\\n107:     return tuple(out)\\n108: \\n109: \\n110: class nd_grid:\\n111:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n112:     Construct a multi-dimensional \\\"meshgrid\\\".\\n113: \\n114:     ``grid = nd_grid()`` creates an instance which will return a mesh-grid\\n115:     when indexed.  The dimension and number of the output arrays are equal\\n116:     to the number of indexing dimensions.  If the step length is not a\\n117:     complex number, then the stop is not inclusive.\\n118: \\n119:     However, if the step length is a **complex number** (e.g. 5j), then the\\n120:     integer part of its magnitude is interpreted as specifying the\\n121:     number of points to create between the start and stop values, where\\n122:     the stop value **is inclusive**.\\n123: \\n124:     If instantiated with an argument of ``sparse=True``, the mesh-grid is\\n125:     open (or not fleshed out) so that only one-dimension of each returned\\n126:     argument is greater than 1.\\n127: \\n128:     Parameters\\n129:     ----------\\n130:     sparse : bool, optional\\n131:         Whether the grid is sparse or not. Default is False.\\n132: \\n133:     Notes\\n134:     -----\\n135:     Two instances of `nd_grid` are made available in the NumPy namespace,\\n136:     `mgrid` and `ogrid`, approximately defined as::\\n137: \\n138:         mgrid = nd_grid(sparse=False)\\n139:         ogrid = nd_grid(sparse=True)\\n140: \\n141:     Users should use these pre-defined instances instead of using `nd_grid`\\n142:     directly.\\n143:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n144:     __slots__ = ('sparse',)\\n145: \\n146:     def __init__(self, sparse=False):\\n147:         self.sparse = sparse\\n148: \\n149:     def __getitem__(self, key):\\n150:         try:\\n151:             size = []\\n152:             # Mimic the behavior of `np.arange` and use a data type\\n153:             # which is at least as large as `np.int_`\\n154:             num_list = [0]\\n155:             for k in range(len(key)):\\n156:                 step = key[k].step\\n157:                 start = key[k].start\\n158:                 stop = key[k].stop\\n159:                 if start is None:\\n160:                     start = 0\\n161:                 if step is None:\\n162:                     step = 1\\n163:                 if isinstance(step, (_nx.complexfloating, complex)):\\n164:                     step = abs(step)\\n165:                     size.append(int(step))\\n166:                 else:\\n167:                     size.append(\\n168:                         int(math.ceil((stop - start) / (step*1.0))))\\n169:                 num_list += [start, stop, step]\\n170:             typ = _nx.result_type(*num_list)\\n171:             if self.sparse:\\n172:                 nn = [_nx.arange(_x, dtype=_t)\\n173:                       for _x, _t in zip(size, (typ,)*len(size))]\\n174:             else:\\n175:                 nn = _nx.indices(size, typ)\\n176:             for k, kk in enumerate(key):\\n177:                 step = kk.step\\n178:                 start = kk.start\\n179:                 if start is None:\\n180:                     start = 0\\n181:                 if step is None:\\n182:                     step = 1\\n183:                 if isinstance(step, (_nx.complexfloating, complex)):\\n184:                     step = int(abs(step))\\n185:                     if step != 1:\\n186:                         step = (kk.stop - start) / float(step - 1)\\n187:                 nn[k] = (nn[k]*step+start)\\n188:             if self.sparse:\\n189:                 slobj = [_nx.newaxis]*len(size)\\n190:                 for k in range(len(size)):\\n191:                     slobj[k] = slice(None, None)\\n192:                     nn[k] = nn[k][tuple(slobj)]\\n193:                     slobj[k] = _nx.newaxis\\n194:                 return tuple(nn)  # ogrid -> tuple of arrays\\n195:             return nn  # mgrid -> ndarray\\n196:         except (IndexError, TypeError):\\n197:             step = key.step\\n198:             stop = key.stop\\n199:             start = key.start\\n200:             if start is None:\\n201:                 start = 0\\n202:             if isinstance(step, (_nx.complexfloating, complex)):\\n203:                 # Prevent the (potential) creation of integer arrays\\n204:                 step_float = abs(step)\\n205:                 step = length = int(step_float)\\n206:                 if step != 1:\\n207:                     step = (key.stop-start)/float(step-1)\\n208:                 typ = _nx.result_type(start, stop, step_float)\\n209:                 return _nx.arange(0, length, 1, dtype=typ)*step + start\\n210:             else:\\n211:                 return _nx.arange(start, stop, step)\\n212: \\n213: \\n214: class MGridClass(nd_grid):\\n215:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n216:     An instance which returns a dense multi-dimensional \\\"meshgrid\\\".\\n217: \\n218:     An instance which returns a dense (or fleshed out) mesh-grid\\n219:     when indexed, so that each returned argument has the same shape.\\n220:     The dimensions and number of the output arrays are equal to the\\n221:     number of indexing dimensions.  If the step length is not a complex\\n222:     number, then the stop is not inclusive.\\n223: \\n224:     However, if the step length is a **complex number** (e.g. 5j), then\\n225:     the integer part of its magnitude is interpreted as specifying the\\n226:     number of points to create between the start and stop values, where\\n227:     the stop value **is inclusive**.\\n228: \\n229:     Returns\\n230:     -------\\n231:     mesh-grid : ndarray\\n232:         A single array, containing a set of `ndarray`\\\\\\\\ s all of the same\\n233:         dimensions. stacked along the first axis.\\n234: \\n235:     See Also\\n236:     --------\\n237:     ogrid : like `mgrid` but returns open (not fleshed out) mesh grids\\n238:     meshgrid: return coordinate matrices from coordinate vectors\\n239:     r_ : array concatenator\\n240:     :ref:`how-to-partition`\\n241: \\n242:     Examples\\n243:     --------\\n244:     >>> import numpy as np\\n245:     >>> np.mgrid[0:5, 0:5]\\n246:     array([[[0, 0, 0, 0, 0],\\n247:             [1, 1, 1, 1, 1],\\n248:             [2, 2, 2, 2, 2],\\n249:             [3, 3, 3, 3, 3],\\n250:             [4, 4, 4, 4, 4]],\\n251:            [[0, 1, else:\\n380:                     newobj = _nx.arange(start, stop, step)\\n381:                 if ndmin > 1:\\n382:                     newobj = array(newobj, copy=None, ndmin=ndmin)\\n383:                     if trans1d != -1:\\n384:                         newobj = newobj.swapaxes(-1, trans1d)\\n385:             elif isinstance(item, str):\\n386:                 if k != 0:\\n387: (ValueError, TypeError) as e:\\n408:                     raise ValueError(\\\"unknown special directive\\\") from e\\n409:             elif type(item) in ScalarType:\\n410:                 scalar = True\\n411:                 newobj = item\\n412:             else:\\n413:                 item_ndim = np.ndim(item)\\n414:                 newobj = array(item, copy=None, subok=True, ndmin=ndmin)\\n415:                 if trans1d != -1 and item_ndim < ndmin:\\n416:                     k2 = ndmin - item_ndim\\n417:                     k1 = trans1d\\n418:                     if k1 < r_ = concatenator(0),\\n451: # etc. because otherwise we couldn't get the doc string to come out right\\n452: # in help(r_)\\n453: \\n454: \\n455: class RClass(AxisConcatenator):\\n456:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n457:     Translates slice objects to concatenation along the first axis.\\n458: \\n459:     This is a simple way to build up arrays quickly. There are two use cases.\\n460: \\n461:     1. If the index expression contains comma separated arrays, then stack\\n462:        them along their first axis.\\n463:     2. If the index expression contains slice notation or scalars then create\\n464:        a 1-D array with a range indicated by the slice notation.\\n465: \\n466:     If slice notation is used, the syntax ``start:stop:step`` is equivalent\\n467:     to ``np.arange(start, stop, step)`` inside of the brackets. However, if\\n468:     ``step`` is an imaginary number (i.e. 100j) then its integer portion is\\n469:     interpreted as a number-of-points desired and the start and stop are\\n470:     inclusive. In other words ``start:stop:stepj`` is interpreted as\\n471:     ``np.linspace(start, stop, step, endpoint=1)`` inside of the brackets.\\n472:     After expansion of slice notation, all comma separated sequences are\\n473:     concatenated together.\\n474: \\n475:     Optional character strings placed as the first element of the index\\n476:     expression can be used to change the output. The strings 'r' or 'c' result\\n477:     in matrix output. If the result is 1-D and 'r' is specified a 1 x N (row)\\n478:     matrix is produced. If the result is 1-D and 'c' is specified, then a N x 1\\n479:     (column) matrix is produced. If the result is 2-D then both provide the\\n480:     same matrix result.\\n481: \\n482:     A string integer specifies which axis to stack multiple comma separated\\n483:     arrays along. A string of two comma-separated integers allows indication\\n484:     of the minimum number of dimensions to force each entry into as the\\n485:     second integer (the axis to concatenate along is still the first integer).\\n486: \\n487:     A string with three comma-separated integers allows specification of the\\n488:     axis to concatenate along, the minimum number of dimensions to force the\\n489:     entries to, and which axis should contain the start of the arrays which\\n490:     are less than the specified number of dimensions. In other words the third\\n491:     integer allows you to specify where the 1's should be placed in the shape\\n492:     of the arrays that have their shapes upgraded. By default, they are placed\\n493:     in the front of the shape tuple. The third argument allows you to specify\\n494:     where the start of the array should be instead. Thus, a third argument of\\n495:     '0' would place the 1's at the end of the array shape. Negative integers\\n496:     specify where in the new shape tuple the last dimension of upgraded arrays\\n497:     should be placed, so the default is '-1'.\\n498: \\n499:     Parameters\\n500:     ----------\\n501:     Not a function, so takes no parameters\\n502: \\n503: \\n504:     Returns\\n505:     -------\\n506:     A concatenated ndarray or matrix.\\n507: \\n508:     See Also\\n509:     --------\\n510:     concatenate : Join a sequence of arrays along an existing axis.\\n511:     c_ : Translates slice objects to concatenation along the second axis.\\n512: \\n513:     Examples\\n514:     --------\\n515:     >>> import numpy as np\\n516:     >>> np.r_[np.array([1,2,3]), 0, 0, np.array([4,5,6])]\\n517:     array([1, 2, 3, ..., 4, 5, 6])\\n518:     >>> np.r_[-1:1:6j, [0]*3, 5, 6]\\n519:     array([-1. , -0.6, -0.2,  0.2,  0.6,  1. ,  0. ,  0. ,  0. ,  5. ,  6. ])\\n520: \\n521:     String integers specify the axis to concatenate along or the minimum\\n522:     number of dimensions to force entries into.\\n523: \\n524:     >>> a = np.array([[0, 1, 2], [3, 4, 5]])\\n525:     >>> np.r_['-1', a, a] # concatenate along last axis\\n526:     array([[0, 1, 2, 0, 1, 2],\\n527:            [3, 4, 5, 3, 4, 5]])\\n528:     >>> np.r_['0,2', [1,2,3], [4,5,6]] # concatenate along first axis, dim>=2\\n529:     array([[1, 2, 3],\\n530:            [4, 5, 6]])\\n531: \\n532:     >>> np.r_['0,2,0', [1,2,3], [4,5,6]]\\n533:     array([[1],\\n534:            [2],\\n535:            [3],\\n536:            [4],\\n537:            [5],\\n538:            [6]])\\n539:     >>> np.r_['1,2,0', [1,2,3], [4,5,6]]\\n540:     array([[1, 4],\\n541:            [2, 5],\\n542:            [3, 6]])\\n543: \\n544:     Using 'r' or 'c' as a first string argument creates a matrix.\\n545: \\n546:     >>> np.r_['r',[1,2,3], [4,5,6]]\\n547:     matrix([[1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]])\\n548: \\n549:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n550:     __slots__ = ()\\n551: \\n552:     def __init__(self):\\n553:         AxisConcatenator.__init__(self, 0)\\n554: \\n555: \\n556: r_ = RClass()\\n557: \\n558: \\n559: class CClass(AxisConcatenator):\\n560:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n561:     Translates slice objects to concatenation along the second axis.\\n562: \\n563:     This is short-hand for ``np.r_['-1,2,0', index expression]``, which is\\n564:     useful because of its common occurrence. In particular, arrays will be\\n565:     stacked along their last axis after being upgraded to at least 2-D with\\n566:     1's post-pended to the shape (column vectors made out of 1-D arrays).\\n567: \\n568:     See Also\\n569:     --------\\n570:     column_stack : Stack 1-D arrays as columns into a 2-D array.\\n571:     r_ : For more detailed documentation.\\n572: \\n573:     Examples\\n574:     --------\\n575:     >>> import numpy as np\\n576:     >>> np.c_[np.array([1,2,3]), np.array([4,5,6])]\\n577:     array([[1, 4],\\n578:            [2, 5],\\n579:            [3, 6]])\\n580:     >>> np.c_[np.array([[1,2,3]]), 0, 0, np.array([[4,5,6]])]\\n581:     array([[1, 2, 3, ..., 4, 5, 6]])\\n582: \\n583:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n584:     __slots__ = ()\\n585: \\n586:     def     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n596:     Multidimensional index iterator.\\n597: \\n598:     Return an iterator yielding pairs of array coordinates and values.\\n599: \\n600:     Parameters\\n601: ndindex:\\n645:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n646:     An N-dimensional iterator object to index arrays.\\n647: \\n648:     Given the shape of an array, an `ndindex` instance iterates over\\n649:     the N-dimensional index of the array. At each iteration a tuple\\n650:     of indices is returned, the last dimension is iterated over first.\\n651: \\n652:     Parameters\\n653:     ----------\\n654:     shape : ints, or a single tuple --------\\n660:     ndenumerate, flatiter\\n661: \\n662:     Examples\\n663:     --------\\n664:     >>> import numpy as np\\n665: \\n666:     Dimensions as individual arguments\\n667: \\n668:     >>> \\n677:     Same dimensions - but in a tuple ``(3, 2, 1)``\\n678: \\n679:     >>> for index in np.ndindex((3, 2, 1)):\\n680:     ...     print(index)\\n681:     (0, 0, 0)\\n682:     (0, 1, 0)\\n683:     (1, 0, 0)\\n684:     (1, 1, 0)\\n685:     (2, 0, 0)\\n686:     (2, 1, 0)\\n687: \\n688:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n689: \\n690:     def __init__(self, *shape):\\n691:         if len(shape) == 1 and isinstance(shape[0], tuple):\\n692:             shape = shape[0]\\n693:         x = as_strided(_nx.zeros(1), shape=shape,\\n694:                        strides=_nx.zeros_like(shape))\\n695:         self._it = _nx.nditer(x, flags=['multi_index', 'zerosize_ok'],\\n696:                               order='C')\\n697: \\n698:     def __iter__(self):\\n699:         return self\\n700: \\n701:     def ndincr(self):\\n702:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n703:         Increment the multi-dimensional index by one.\\n704: \\n705:         This method is for backward compatibility only: do not use.\\n706: \\n707:         .. deprecated:: 1.20.0\\n708:             This method has been advised against since numpy 1.8.0, but only\\n709:             started emitting DeprecationWarning as of this version.\\n710:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n711:         # NumPy 1.20.0, 2020-09-08\\n712:         warnings.warn(\\n713:             \\\"`ndindex.ndincr()` is deprecated, use `next(ndindex)` instead\\\",\\n714:             DeprecationWarning, stacklevel=2)\\n715:         next(self)\\n716: \\n717:     def __next__(self):\\n718:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n719:         Standard iterator method, updates the index and returns the index\\n720:         tuple.\\n721: \\n722:         Returns\\n723:         -------\\n724:         val : tuple of ints\\n725:             Returns a tuple containing the indices of the current\\n726:             iteration.\\n727: \\n728:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n729:         next(self._it)\\n730:         return self._it.multi_index\\n731: \\n732: \\n733: # You can do all this with slice() plus a few special objects,\\n734: # but there's a lot to remember. This version is simpler because\\n735: # it uses the standard array indexing syntax.\\n736: #\\n737: # Written by Konrad Hinsen <hinsen@cnrs-orleans.fr>\\n738: # last revision: 1999-7-23\\n739: #\\n740: # Cosmetic changes by T. Oliphant 2001\\n741: #\\n742: #\\n743: \\n744: class IndexExpression:\\n745:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n746:     A nicer way to build up index tuples for arrays.\\n747: \\n748:     .. note::\\n749:        Use one of the two predefined instances ``index_exp`` or `s_`\\n750:        rather than directly using `IndexExpression`.\\n751: \\n752:     For any index combination, including slicing and axis insertion,\\n753:     ``a[indices]`` is the same as ``a[np.index_exp[indices]]`` for any\\n754:     array `a`. However, ``np.index_exp[indices]`` can be used anywhere\\n755:     in Python code and returns a tuple of slice objects that can be\\n756:     used in the construction of complex index expressions.\\n757: \\n758:     Parameters\\n759:     ----------\\n760:     maketuple : bool\\n761:         If True, always returns a tuple.\\n762: \\n763:     See Also\\n764:     --------\\n765:     s_ : Predefined instance without tuple conversion:\\n766:        `s_ = IndexExpression(maketuple=False)`.\\n767:        The ``index_exp`` is another predefined instance that\\n768:        always returns a tuple:\\n769:        `index_exp = IndexExpression(maketuple=True)`.\\n770: \\n771:     Notes\\n772:     -----\\n773:     You can do all this with :class:`slice` plus a few special objects,\\n774:     but there's a lot to remember and this version is simpler because\\n775:     it uses the standard array indexing syntax.\\n776: \\n777:     Examples\\n778:     --------\\n779:     >>> import numpy as np\\n780:     >>> np.s_[2::2]\\n781:     slice(2, None, 2)\\n782:     >>> np.index_exp[2::2]\\n783:     (slice(2, None, 2),)\\n784: \\n785:     >>> np.array([0, 1, 2, 3, 4])[np.s_[2::2]]\\n786:     array([2, 4])\\n787: \\n788:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n789:     __slots__ = ('maketuple',)\\n790: \\n791:     def __init__(self, maketuple):\\n792:         self.maketuple = maketuple\\n793: \\n794:     def __getitem__(self, item):\\n795:         if self.maketuple and not isinstance(item, tuple):\\n796:             return (item,)\\n797:         else:\\n798:             return item\\n799: \\n800: \\n801: index_exp = IndexExpression(maketuple=True)\\n802: s_ = IndexExpression(maketuple=False)\\n803: \\n804: # End contribution from Konrad.\\n805: \\n806: \\n807: # The following functions complement those in twodim_base, but are\\n808: # applicable to N-dimensions.\\n809: \\n810: \\n811: def _fill_diagonal_dispatcher(a, val, wrap=None):\\n812:     return (a,)\\n813: \\n814: \\n815: @array_function_dispatch(_fill_diagonal_dispatcher)\\n816: def fill_diagonal(a, val, wrap=False):\\n817:     \\\"\\\"\\\"Fill the main diagonal of the given array of any dimensionality.\\n818: \\n819:     For an array `a` with ``a.ndim >= 2``, the diagonal is the list of\\n820:     values ``a[i, ..., i]`` with indices ``i`` all identical.  This function\\n821:     modifies the input array in-place without returning a value.\\n822: \\n823:     Parameters\\n824:     ----------\\n825:     a : array, at least 2-D.\\n826:       Array whose diagonal is to be filled in-place.\\n827:     val : scalar or array_like\\n828:       Value(s) to write on the diagonal. If `val` is scalar, the value is\\n829:       written along the diagonal. If array-like, the flattened `val` is\\n830:       written along the diagonal, repeating if necessary to fill all\\n831:       diagonal entries.\\n832: \\n833:     wrap : bool\\n834:       For tall matrices in NumPy version up to 1.6.2, the\\n835:       diagonal \\\"wrapped\\\" after N columns. You can have this behavior\\n836:       with this option. This affects only tall matrices.\\n837: \\n838:     See also\\n839:     --------\\n840:     diag_indices, diag_indices_from\\n841: \\n842:     Notes\\n843:     -----\\n844:     This functionality can be obtained via `diag_indices`, but internally\\n845:     this version uses a much faster implementation that never constructs the\\n846:     indices and uses simple slicing.\\n847: \\n848:     Examples\\n849:     --------\\n850:     >>> import numpy as np\\n851:     >>> a = np.zeros((3, 3), int)\\n852:     >>> np.fill_diagonal(a, 5)\\n853:     >>> a\\n854:     array([[5, 0, 0],\\n855:            [0, 5, 0],\\n856:            [0, 0, 5]])\\n857: \\n858:     The same function can operate on a 4-D array:\\n859: \\n860:     >>> a = np.zeros((3, 3, 3, 3), int)\\n861:     >>> np.fill_diagonal(a, 4)\\n862: \\n863:     We only show a few blocks for clarity:\\n864: \\n865:     >>> a[0, 0]\\n866:     array([[4, 0, 0],\\n867:            [0, 0, 0],\\n868:            [0, 0, 0]])\\n869:     >>> a[1, 1]\\n870:     array([[0, 0, 0],\\n871:            [0, 4, 0],\\n872:            [0, 0, 0]])\\n873:     >>> a[2, 2]\\n874:     array([[0, 0, 0],\\n875:            [0, 0, 0],\\n876:            [0, 0, 4]])\\n877: \\n878:     The wrap option affects only tall matrices:\\n879: \\n880:     >>> # tall matrices no wrap\\n881:     >>> a = np.zeros((5, 3), int)\\n882:     >>> np.fill_diagonal(a, 4)\\n883:     >>> a\\n884:     array([[4, 0, 0],\\n885:            [0, 4, 0],\\n886:            [0, 0, 4],\\n887:            [0, 0, 0],\\n888:            [0, 0, 0]])\\n889: \\n890:     >>> # tall matrices wrap\\n891:     >>> a = np.zeros((5, 3), int)\\n892:     >>> np.fill_diagonal(a, 4, wrap=True)\\n893:     >>> a\\n894:     array([[4, 0, 0],\\n895:            [0, 4, 0],\\n896:            [0, 0, 4],\\n897:            [0, 0, 0],\\n898:            [4, 0, 0]])\\n899: \\n900:     >>> # wide matrices\\n901:     >>> a = np.zeros((3, 5), int)\\n902:     >>> np.fill_diagonal(a, 4, wrap=True)\\n903:     >>> a\\n904:     array([[4, 0, 0, 0, 0],\\n905:            [0, 4, 0, 0, 0],\\n906:            [0, 0, 4, 0, 0]])\\n907: \\n908:     The anti-diagonal can be filled by reversing the order of elements\\n909:     using either `numpy.flipud` or `numpy.fliplr`.\\n910: \\n911:     >>> a = np.zeros((3, 3), int);\\n912:     >>> np.fill_diagonal(np.fliplr(a), [1,2,3])  # Horizontal flip\\n913:     >>> a\\n914:     array([[0, 0, 1],\\n915:            [0, 2, 0],\\n916:            [3, 0, 0]])\\n917:     >>> np.fill_diagonal(np.flipud(a), [1,2,3])  # Vertical flip\\n918:     >>> a\\n919:     array([[0, 0, 3],\\n920:            [0, 2, 0],\\n921:            [1, 0, 0]])\\n922: \\n923:     Note that the order in which the diagonal is filled varies depending\\n924:     on the flip function.\\n925:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n926:     if a.ndim < 2:\\n927:         raise ValueError(\\\"array must be at least 2-d\\\")\\n928:     end = None\\n929:     if a.ndim == 2:\\n930:         # Explicit, fast formula for the common case.  For 2-d arrays, we\\n931:         # accept rectangular ones.\\n932:         step = a.shape[1] + 1\\n933:         # This is needed to don't have tall matrix have the diagonal wrap.\\n934:         if not wrap:\\n935:             end = a.shape[1] * a.shape[1]\\n936:     else:\\n937:         # For more than d=2, the strided formula is only valid for arrays with\\n938:         # all dimensions equal, so we check first.\\n939:         if not np.all(diff(a.shape) == 0):\\n940:             raise ValueError(\\\"All dimensions of input must be of equal length\\\")\\n941:         step = 1 + (np.cumprod(a.shape[:-1])).sum()\\n942: \\n943:     # Write the value out into the diagonal.\\n944:     a.flat[:end:step] = val\\n945: \\n946: \\n947: @set_module('numpy')\\n948: def diag_indices(n, ndim=2):\\n949:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n950:     Return the indices to access the main diagonal of an array.\\n951: \\n952:     This returns a tuple of indices that can be used to access the main\\n953:     diagonal of an array `a` with ``a.ndim >= 2`` dimensions and shape\\n954:     (n, n, ..., n). For ``a.ndim = 2`` this is the usual diagonal, for\\n955:     ``a.ndim > 2`` this is the set of indices to access ``a[i, i, ..., i]``\\n956:     for ``i = [0..n-1]``.\\n957: \\n958:     Parameters\\n959:     ----------\\n960:     n : int\\n961:       The size, along each dimension, of the arrays for which the returned\\n962:       indices can be used.\\n963: \\n964:     ndim : int, optional\\n965:       The number of dimensions.\\n966: \\n967:     See Also\\n968:     --------\\n969:     diag_indices_from\\n970: \\n971:     Examples\\n972:     --------\\n973:     >>> import numpy as np\\n974: \\n975:     Create a set of indices to access the diagonal of a (4, 4) array:\\n976: \\n977:     >>> di = np.diag_indices(4)\\n978:     >>> di\\n979:     (array([0, 1, 2, 3]), array([0, 1, 2, 3]))\\n980:     >>> a = np.arange(16).reshape(4, 4)\\n981:     >>> a\\n982:     array([[ 0,  1,  2,  3],\\n983:            [ 4,  5,  6,  7],\\n984:            [ 8,  9, 10, 11],\\n985:            [12, 13, 14, 15]])\\n986:     >>> a[di] = 100\\n987:     >>> a\\n988:     array([[100,   1,   2,   3],\\n989:            [  4, 100,   6,   7],\\n990:            [  8,   9, 100,  11],\\n991:            [ 12,  13,  14, 100]])\\n992: \\n993:     Now, we create indices to manipulate a 3-D array:\\n994: \\n995:     >>> d3 = np.diag_indices(2, 3)\\n996:     >>> d3\\n997:     (array([0, 1]), array([0, 1]), array([0, 1]))\\n998: \\n999:     And use it to set the diagonal of an array of zeros to 1:\\n1000: \\n1001: \",\"url\":\"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-evals/numpy--v2.2.2@9ca88bff35bf60de74f9f95e6f90e6b4e0913e78/-/blob/numpy/lib/_index_tricks_impl.py%3FL1-1000\"}"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":"toolu_01VT936ru8Pg7zNCp1ggNs6m","session_id":"1f08b436-d13a-4943-b612-3a8cf648bba4","uuid":"3737b138-160d-4acd-b454-5654ea6e986b"}

The default\\n51:         is ``None``; if provided, it must have the same shape as the\\n52:         expected output and will prevent the allocation of a new array.\\n53: \\n54:     Returns\\n55:     -------\\n56:     y : bool ndarray or True\\n57:         A bool array where ``np.nan`` positions are marked with ``False``\\n58:         and other positions are marked with ``True``. If the type of ``a``\\n59:         is such that it can't possibly contain ``np.nan``, returns ``True``.\\n60:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n61:     # we assume that a is an array for this private function\\n62: \\n63:     if a.dtype.kind not in 'fc':\\n64:         return True\\n65: \\n66:     y = np.isnan(a, out=out)\\n67:     y = np.invert(y, out=y)\\n68:     return y\\n69: \\n70: def _replace_nan(a, val):\\n71:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n72:     If `a` is of inexact type, make a copy of `a`, replace NaNs with\\n73:     the `val` value, and return the copy together with a boolean mask\\n74:     marking the locations where NaNs were present. If `a` is not of\\n75:     inexact type, do nothing and return `a` together with a mask of None.\\n76: \\n77:     Note that scalars will end up as array scalars, which is important\\n78:     for using the result as the value of the out argument in some\\n79:     operations.\\n80: \\n81:     Parameters\\n82:     ----------\\n83:     a : array-like\\n84:         Input array.\\n85:     val : float\\n86:         NaN values are set to val before doing the operation.\\n87: \\n88:     Returns\\n89:     -------\\n90:     y : ndarray\\n91:         If `a` is of inexact type, return a copy of `a` with the NaNs\\n92:         replaced by the fill value, otherwise return `a`.\\n93:     mask: {bool, None}\\n94:         If `a` is of inexact type, return a boolean mask marking locations of\\n95:         NaNs, otherwise return None.\\n96: \\n97:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n98:     a = np.asanyarray(a)\\n99: \\n100:     if a.dtype == np.object_:\\n101:         # object arrays do not support `isnan` (gh-9009), so make a guess\\n102:         mask = np.not_equal(a, a, dtype=bool)\\n103:     elif issubclass(a.dtype.type, np.inexact):\\n104:         mask = np.isnan(a)\\n105:     else:\\n106:         mask = None\\n107: \\n108:     if mask is not None:\\n109:         a = np.array(a, subok=True, copy=True)\\n110:         np.copyto(a, val, where=mask)\\n111: \\n112:     return a, mask\\n113: \\n114: \\n115: def _copyto(a, val, mask):\\n116:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n117:     Replace values in `a` with NaN where `mask` is True.  This differs from\\n118:     copyto in that it will deal with the case where `a` is a numpy scalar.\\n119: \\n120:     Parameters\\n121:     ----------\\n122:     a : ndarray or numpy scalar\\n123:         Array or numpy scalar some of whose values are to be replaced\\n124:         by val.\\n125:     val : numpy scalar\\n126:         Value used a replacement.\\n127:     mask : ndarray, scalar\\n128:         Boolean array. Where True the corresponding element of `a` is\\n129:         replaced by `val`. Broadcasts.\\n130: \\n131:     Returns\\n132:     -------\\n133:     res : ndarray, scalar\\n134:         Array with elements replaced or scalar `val`.\\n135: \\n136:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n137:     if isinstance(a, np.ndarray):\\n138:         np.copyto(a, val, where=mask, casting='unsafe')\\n139:     else:\\n140:         a = a.dtype.type(val)\\n141:     return a\\n142: \\n143: \\n144: def _remove_nan_1d(arr1d, second_arr1d=None, overwrite_input=False):\\n145:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n146:     Equivalent to arr1d[~arr1d.isnan()], but in a different order\\n147: \\n148:     Presumably faster as it incurs fewer copies\\n149: \\n150:     Parameters\\n151:     ----------\\n152:     arr1d : ndarray\\n153:         Array to remove nans from\\n154:     second_arr1d : ndarray or None\\n155:         A second array which will have the same positions removed as arr1d.\\n156:     overwrite_input : bool\\n157:         True if `arr1d` can be modified in place\\n158: \\n159:     Returns\\n160:     -------\\n161:     res : ndarray\\n162:         Array with nan elements removed\\n163:     second_res : ndarray or None\\n164:         Second array with nan element positions of first array removed.\\n165:     overwrite_input : bool\\n166:         True if `res` can be modified in place, given the constraint on the\\n167:         input\\n168:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n169:     if arr1d.dtype == object:\\n170:         # object arrays do not support `isnan` (gh-9009), so make a guess\\n171:         c = np.not_equal(arr1d, arr1d, dtype=bool)\\n172:     else:\\n173:         c = np.isnan(arr1d)\\n174: \\n175:     s = np.nonzero(c)[0]\\n176:     if s.size == arr1d.size:\\n177:         warnings.warn(\\\"All-NaN slice encountered\\\", RuntimeWarning,\\n178:                       stacklevel=6)\\n179:         if second_arr1d is None:\\n180:             return arr1d[:0], None, True\\n181:         else:\\n182:             return arr1d[:0], second_arr1d[:0], True\\n183:     elif s.size == 0:\\n184:         return arr1d, second_arr1d, overwrite_input\\n185:     else:\\n186:         if not overwrite_input:\\n187:             arr1d = arr1d.copy()\\n188:         # select non-nans at end of array\\n189:         enonan = arr1d[-s.size:][~c[-s.size:]]\\n190:         # fill nans in beginning of array with non-nans of end\\n191:         arr1d[s[:enonan.size]] = enonan\\n192: \\n193:         if second_arr1d is None:\\n194:             return arr1d[:-s.size], None, True\\n195:         else:\\n196:             if not overwrite_input:\\n197:                 second_arr1d = second_arr1d.copy()\\n198:             enonan = second_arr1d[-s.size:][~c[-s.size:]]\\n199:             second_arr1d[s[:enonan.size]] = enonan\\n200: \\n201:             return arr1d[:-s.size], second_arr1d[:-s.size], True\\n202: \\n203: \\n204: def _divide_by_count(a, b, out=None):\\n205:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n206:     Compute a/b ignoring invalid results. If `a` is an array the division\\n207:     is done in place. If `a` is a scalar, then its type is preserved in the\\n208:     output. If out is None, then a is used instead so that the division\\n209:     is in place. Note that this is only called with `a` an inexact type.\\n210: \\n211:     Parameters\\n212:     ----------\\n213:     a : {ndarray, numpy scalar}\\n214:         Numerator. Expected to be of inexact type but not checked.\\n215:     b : {ndarray, numpy scalar}\\n216:         Denominator.\\n217:     out : ndarray, optional\\n218:         Alternate output array in which to place the result.  The default\\n219:         is ``None``; if provided, it must have the same shape as the\\n220:         expected output, but the type will be cast if necessary.\\n221: \\n222:     Returns\\n223:     -------\\n224:     ret : {ndarray, numpy scalar}\\n225:         The return value is a/b. If `a` was an ndarray the division is done\\n226:         in place. If `a` is a numpy scalar, the division preserves its type.\\n227: \\n228:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n229:     with np.errstate(invalid='ignore', divide='ignore'):\\n230:         if isinstance(a, np.ndarray):\\n231:             if out is None:\\n232:                 return np.divide(a, b, out=a, casting='unsafe')\\n233:             else:\\n234:                 return np.divide(a, b, out=out, casting='unsafe')\\n235:         else:\\n236:             if out is None:\\n237:                 # Precaution against reduced object arrays\\n238:                 try:\\n239:                     return a.dtype.type(a / b)\\n240:                 except AttributeError:\\n241:                     return a / b\\n242:             else:\\n243:                 # This is questionable, but currently a numpy scalar can\\n244:                 # be output to a zero dimensional array.\\n245:                 return np.divide(a, b, out=out, casting='unsafe')\\n246: \\n247: \\n248: def _nanmin_dispatcher(a, axis=None, out=None, keepdims=None,\\n249:                        initial=None, where=None):\\n250:     return (a, out)\\n251: \\n252: \\n253: @array_function_dispatch(_nanmin_dispatcher)\\n254: def nanmin(a, axis=None, out=None, keepdims=np._NoValue, initial=np._NoValue,\\n255:            where=np._NoValue):\\n256:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n257:     Return minimum of an array or minimum along an axis, ignoring any NaNs.\\n258:     When all-NaN slices are encountered a ``RuntimeWarning`` is raised and\\n259:     Nan is returned for that slice.\\n260: \\n261:     Parameters\\n262:     ----------\\n263:     a : array_like\\n264:         Array containing numbers whose minimum is desired. If `a` is not an\\n265:         array, a conversion is attempted.\\n266:     axis : {int, tuple of int, None}, optional\\n267:         Axis or axes along which the minimum is computed. The default is to compute\\n268:         the minimum of the flattened array.\\n269:     out : ndarray, optional\\n270:         Alternate output array in which to place the result.  The default\\n271:         is ``None``; if provided, it must have the same shape as the\\n272:         expected output, but the type will be cast if necessary. See\\n273:         :ref:`ufuncs-output-type` for more details.\\n274:     keepdims : bool, optional\\n275:         If this is set to True, the axes which are reduced are left\\n276:         in the result as dimensions with size one. With this option,\\n277:         the result will broadcast correctly against the original `a`.\\n278: \\n279:         If the value is anything but the default, then\\n280:         `keepdims` will be passed through to the `min` method\\n281:         of sub-classes of `ndarray`.  If the sub-classes methods\\n282:         does not implement `keepdims` any exceptions will be raised.\\n283:     initial : scalar, optional\\n284:         The maximum value of an output element. Must be present to allow\\n285:         computation on empty slice. See `~numpy.ufunc.reduce` for details.\\n286: \\n287:         .. versionadded:: 1.22.0\\n288:     where : array_like of bool, optional\\n289:         Elements to compare for the minimum. See `~numpy.ufunc.reduce`\\n290:         for details.\\n291: \\n292:         .. versionadded:: 1.22.0\\n293: \\n294:     Returns\\n295:     -------\\n296:     nanmin : ndarray\\n297:         An array with the same shape as `a`, with the specified axis\\n298:         removed.  If `a` is a 0-d array, or if axis is None, an ndarray\\n299:         scalar is returned.  The same dtype as `a` is returned.\\n300: \\n301:     See Also\\n302:     --------\\n303:     nanmax :\\n304:         The maximum value of an array along a given axis, ignoring any NaNs.\\n305:     amin :\\n306:         The minimum value of an array along a given axis, propagating any NaNs.\\n307:     fmin :\\n308:         Element-wise minimum of two arrays, ignoring any NaNs.\\n309:     minimum :\\n310:         Element-wise minimum of two arrays, propagating any NaNs.\\n311:     isnan :\\n312:         Shows which elements are Not a Number (NaN).\\n313:     isfinite:\\n314:         Shows which elements are neither NaN nor infinity.\\n315: \\n316:     amax, fmax, maximum\\n317: \\n318:     Notes\\n319:     -----\\n320:     NumPy uses the IEEE Standard for Binary Floating-Point for Arithmetic\\n321:     (IEEE 754). This means that Not a Number is not equivalent to infinity.\\n322:     Positive infinity is treated as a very large number and negative\\n323:     infinity is treated as a very small (i.e. negative) number.\\n324: \\n325:     If the input has a integer type the function is equivalent to np.min.\\n326: \\n327:     Examples\\n328:     --------\\n329:     >>> import numpy as np\\n330:     >>> a = np.array([[1, 2], [3, np.nan]])\\n331:     >>> np.nanmin(a)\\n332:     1.0\\n333:     >>> np.nanmin(a, axis=0)\\n334:     array([1.,  2.])\\n335:     >>> np.nanmin(a, axis=1)\\n336:     array([1.,  3.])\\n337: \\n338:     When positive infinity and negative infinity are present:\\n339: \\n340:     >>> np.nanmin([1, 2, np.nan, np.inf])\\n341:     1.0\\n342:     >>> np.nanmin([1, 2, np.nan, -np.inf])\\n343:     -inf\\n344: \\n345:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n346:     kwargs = {}\\n347:     if keepdims is not np._NoValue:\\n348:         kwargs['keepdims'] = keepdims\\n349:     if initial is not np._NoValue:\\n350:         kwargs['initial'] = initial\\n351:     if where is not np._NoValue:\\n352:         kwargs['where'] = where\\n353: \\n354:     if type(a) is np.ndarray and a.dtype != np.object_:\\n355:         # Fast, but not safe for subclasses of ndarray, or object arrays,\\n356:         # which do not implement isnan (gh-9009), or fmin correctly (gh-8975)\\n357:         res = np.fmin.reduce(a, axis=axis, out=out, **kwargs)\\n358:         if np.isnan(res).any():\\n359:             warnings.warn(\\\"All-NaN slice encountered\\\", RuntimeWarning,\\n360:                           stacklevel=2)\\n361:     else:\\n362:         # Slow, but safe for subclasses of ndarray\\n363:         a, mask = _replace_nan(a, +np.inf)\\n364:         res = np.amin(a, axis=axis, out=out, **kwargs)\\n365:         if mask is None:\\n366:             return res\\n367: \\n368:         # Check for all-NaN axis\\n369:         kwargs.pop(\\\"initial\\\", None)\\n370:         mask = np.all(mask, axis=axis, **kwargs)\\n371:         if np.any(mask):\\n372:             res = _copyto(res, np.nan, mask)\\n373:             warnings.warn(\\\"All-NaN axis encountered\\\", RuntimeWarning,\\n374:                           stacklevel=2)\\n375:     return res\\n376: \\n377: \\n378: def _nanmax_dispatcher(a, axis=None, out=None, keepdims=None,\\n379:                        initial=None, where=None):\\n380:     return (a, out)\\n381: \\n382: \\n383: @array_function_dispatch(_nanmax_dispatcher)\\n384: def nanmax(a, axis=None, out=None, keepdims=np._NoValue, initial=np._NoValue,\\n385:            where=np._NoValue):\\n386:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n387:     Return the maximum of an array or maximum along an axis, ignoring any\\n388:     NaNs. In later versions zero is returned.\\n644: \\n645:     Parameters\\n646:     ----------\\n647:     a : array_like\\n648:         Array containing numbers whose sum is desired. If `a` is not an\\n649:         array, a conversion is attempted.\\n650:     axis : {int, tuple of int, None}, optional\\n651:         Axis or axes along which the sum is computed. The default is to compute the\\n652:         sum of the flattened array.\\n653:     dtype : data-type, optional\\n654:         The type of the returned array and of the accumulator in which the\\n655:         elements are summed.  By default, the dtype of `a` is used.  An\\n656:         exception is when `a` has an integer type with less precision than\\n657:         the platform (u)intp. In that case, the default will be either\\n658:         (u)int32 or (u)int64 depending on whether the platform is 32 or 64\\n659:         bits. For inexact inputs, dtype must be inexact.\\n660:     out : ndarray, optional\\n661:         Alternate output array in which to place the result.  The default\\n662:         is ``None``. If provided, it must have the same shape as the\\n663:         expected output, but the type will be cast if necessary.  See\\n664:         :ref:`ufuncs-output-type` for more details. The casting of NaN to integer\\n665:         can yield unexpected results.\\n666:     keepdims : bool, optional\\n667:         If this is set to True, the axes which are reduced are left\\n668:         in the result as dimensions with size one. With this option,\\n669:         the result will broadcast correctly against the original `a`.\\n670: \\n671:         If the value is anything but the default, then\\n672:         `keepdims` will be passed through to the `mean` or `sum` methods\\n673:         of sub-classes of `ndarray`. The result has the same\\n851:         size as `a`, and the same shape as `a` if `axis` is not None\\n852:         or `a` is a 1-d array.\\n853: \\n854:     See Also\\n855:     --------\\n856:     numpy.cumsum : Cumulative sum across array propagating NaNs.\\n857:     isnan : Show which elements are NaN.\\n858: \\n859:     Examples\\n860:     --------\\n861:     >>> import numpy as np\\n862:     >>> np.nancumsum(1)\\n863:     array([1])\\n864:     >>> np.nancumsum([1])\\n865:     array([1])\\n866:     >>> np.nancumsum([1, np.nan])\\n867:     array([1.,  1.])\\n868:     >>> a = np.array([[1, 2], [3, np.nan]])\\n869:     >>> np.nancumsum(a)\\n870:     array([1.,  3.,  6.,  6.])\\n871:     >>> np.nancumsum(a, axis=0)\\n872:     array([[1.,  2.],\\n873:            [4.,  2.]])\\n874:     >>> np.nancumsum(a, axis=1)\\n875:     array([[1.,  3.],\\n876:            [3.,  3.]])\\n877: \\n878:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n879:     a, mask = _replace_nan(a, 0)\\n880:     return np.cumsum(a, axis=axis, dtype=dtype, out=out)\\n881: \\n882: \\n883: def _nancumprod_dispatcher(a, axis=None, dtype=None, out=None):\\n884:     return (a, out)\\n885: \\n886: \\n887: @array_function_dispatch(_nancumprod_dispatcher)\\n888: def nancumprod(a, axis=None, dtype=None, out=None):\\n889:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n890:     Return the cumulative product of array elements over a given axis treating Not a\\n891:     Numbers (NaNs) as one.  The cumulative product does not change when NaNs are\\n892:     encountered and leading NaNs are replaced by ones.\\n893: \\n894:     Ones are returned for slices that are all-NaN or empty.\\n895: \\n896:     Parameters\\n897:     ----------\\n898:     a : array_like\\n899:         Input array.\\n900:     axis : int, optional\\n901:         Axis along which the cumulative product is computed.  By default\\n902:         the input is flattened.\\n903:     dtype : dtype, optional\\n904:         Type of the returned array, as well as of the accumulator in which\\n905:         the elements are multiplied.  If *dtype* is not specified, it\\n906:         defaults to the dtype of `a`, unless `a` has an integer dtype with\\n907:         a precision less than that of the default platform integer.  In\\n908:         that case, the default platform integer is used instead.\\n909:     out : ndarray, optional\\n910:         Alternative output array in which to place the result. It must\\n911:         have the same shape and buffer length as the expected output\\n912:         but the type of the resulting values will be cast if necessary.\\n913: \\n914:     Returns\\n915:     -------\\n916:     nancumprod : ndarray\\n917:         A new array holding the result is returned unless `out` is\\n918:         specified, in which case it is returned.\\n919: \\n920:     See Also\\n921:     --------\\n922:     numpy.cumprod : Cumulative product across array propagating NaNs.\\n923:     isnan : Show which elements are NaN.\\n924: \\n925:     Examples\\n926:     --------\\n927:     >>> import numpy as np\\n928:     >>> np.nancumprod(1)\\n929:     array([1])\\n930:     >>> np.nancumprod([1])\\n931:     array([1])\\n932:     >>> np.nancumprod([1, np.nan])\\n933:     array([1.,  1.])\\n934:     >>> a = np.array([[1, 2], [3, np.nan]])\\n935:     >>> np.nancumprod(a)\\n936:     array([1.,  2.,  6.,  6.])\\n937:     >>> np.nancumprod(a, axis=0)\\n938:     array([[1.,  2.],\\n939:            [3.,  2.]])\\n940:     >>> np.nancumprod(a, axis=1)\\n941:     array([[1.,  2.],\\n942:            [3.,  3.]])\\n943: \\n944:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n945:     a, mask = _replace_nan(a, 1)\\n946:     return np.cumprod(a, axis=axis, dtype=dtype, out=out)\\n947: \\n948: \\n949: def _nanmean_dispatcher(a, axis=None, dtype=None, out=None, keepdims=None,\\n950:                         *, where=None):\\n951:     return (a, out)\\n952: \\n953: \\n954: @array_function_dispatch(_nanmean_dispatcher)\\n955: def nanmean(a, axis=None, dtype=None, out=None, keepdims=np._NoValue,\\n956:             *, where=np._NoValue):\\n957:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n958:     Compute the arithmetic mean along the specified axis, ignoring NaNs.\\n959: \\n960:     Returns the average of the array elements.  The average is taken over\\n961:     the flattened array by default, otherwise over the specified axis.\\n962:     `float64` intermediate and return values are used for integer inputs.\\n963: \\n964:     For all-NaN slices, NaN is returned and a `RuntimeWarning` is raised.\\n965: \\n966:     Parameters\\n967:     ----------\\n968:     a : array_like\\n969:         Array containing numbers whose mean is desired. If `a` is not an\\n970:         array, a conversion is attempted.\\n971:     axis : {int, tuple of int, None}, optional\\n972:         Axis or axes along which the means are computed. The default is to compute\\n973:         the mean of the flattened array.\\n974:     dtype : data-type, optional\\n975:         Type to use in computing the mean.  For integer inputs, the default\\n976:         is `float64`; for inexact inputs, it is the same as the input\\n977:         dtype.\\n978:     out : ndarray, optional\\n979:         Alternate output array in which to place the result.  The default\\n980:         is ``None``; if provided, it must have the same shape as the\\n981:         expected output, but the type will be cast if necessary.\\n982:         See :ref:`ufuncs-output-type` for more details.\\n983:     keepdims : bool, optional\\n984:         If this is set to True, the axes which are reduced are left\\n985:         in the result as dimensions with size one. With this option,\\n986:         the result will broadcast correctly against the original `a`.\\n987: \\n988:         If the value is anything but the default, then\\n989:         `keepdims` will be passed through to the `mean` or `sum` methods\\n990:         of sub-classes of `ndarray`.  If the sub-classes methods\\n991:         does not implement `keepdims` any exceptions will be raised.\\n992:     where : array_like of bool, optional\\n993:         Elements to include in the mean. See `~numpy.ufunc.reduce` for details.\\n994: \\n995:         .. versionadded:: 1.22.0\\n996: \\n997:     Returns\\n998:     -------\\n999:     m : ndarray, see dtype parameter above\\n1000:         If `out=None`, returns a new array containing the mean values,\\n1001: \",\"url\":\"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-evals/numpy--v2.2.2@9ca88bff35bf60de74f9f95e6f90e6b4e0913e78/-/blob/numpy/lib/_nanfunctions_impl.py%3FL1-1000\"}"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":"toolu_01VT936ru8Pg7zNCp1ggNs6m","session_id":"1f08b436-d13a-4943-b612-3a8cf648bba4","uuid":"b2b0a37c-bf60-45fe-bab8-932260bc47d7"}
